18.body: The drawing is new. I notice it while the coffee is running: a house, or something like a house, with a very large garden extending from its base in rows that a child has made precise with careful parallel lines. Green crayon for the garden. Brown for the walls. A sun in the upper right corner, which I recognize as a convention rather than an observation, the sun having never occupied that exact position in any view our daughter has seen from this apartment. I look at it for a moment and I do not know who made it or why it appeared this morning and I set it aside in my head and pour the coffee and sit at the table. The entry lock took two cycles last night. I noted it when I came in from the road at just past midnight, the bicycle locked to the transit rack on the north side of the building, my coat still cold from the forty-minute return through the dead zone, my legs carrying the cost of the day in a specific soreness I would not have been able to describe to anyone at Nexus Corp without explaining where I had been. The first authentication cycle completed and the indicator light held amber. The second cycle, about four seconds later, resolved green. I stood in the hall and noted the new degradation level and noted that I was not surprised by it and I went in. This is what the next tier of flag status produces. Two cycles instead of one. I had already calculated this. It was in the arithmetic I did before I left for Wren's. I am sitting at the chart when Sera comes into the kitchen. She looks at the chart and then at me. This is a new version of a look I have been seeing for three weeks, but it is not identical to the previous versions. Something in it has resolved. I do not know yet in which direction. She pours coffee for herself and sits across from me. Not the chair that faces the window. The chair directly across, the one she sits in when she is not going to get up quickly. "I need to tell you something," I say. "All right," she says. I give it to her the way I gave it to Wren: plainly, without argument, in technical terms. Here is what exists. Here is what it could become. The facility is forty minutes outside Vael's boundary, in a dead zone with no drone coverage and no CDL authentication mesh, an abandoned agricultural processing plant on a land parcel that appears in no current CDL registry because the survey data is fourteen years old and the facility's legal status is one of several hundred thousand parcels that fell outside the managed integration window. Wren has been there for three years. She has soil beds, winter storage, a gravity-fed water system off a hand-pumped well, three generations of germination trial data. She is a physician. She built the intercom herself. Sera is listening. Her hands are around her cup. Her face is doing the thing it does in review sessions, not neutral exactly, the specific quality of attention that means she is logging everything and has not yet sorted it into a response. "Where did you find her," she says. "Pre-integration survey maps. The CDL archive doesn't reach the physical stacks at the city library. Her facility appeared in the topology because the survey was done before the dead zone was classified." "And she's been there three years." Sera is not asking this as a question. She is repeating a data point to verify she has received it correctly. "Yes." "Alone." "Yes." She lifts her cup and sets it down again. She says: "What is her CDL status." "Suspended. She lost her medical license two years ago. Four compliance records before that, all filed in the same window. She was documenting the same pattern I found, in the healthcare allocation module, eighteen months before I identified it from the engineering side." This is the part where her face changes in a way I can read. She was looking for the answer that would make this less serious. The answer to this question does not make it less serious. It makes it more serious because it means I am not the only one, which means the pattern is real enough to produce the same result in a physician working from a different data set. Sera is a behavioral psychologist. She knows what it means when two independent observers arrive at the same finding through different methodologies. She knows I know that she knows this. She does not say it. She says: "What does she want to do with what she's built." "She hasn't defined the objective. She identified a failure mode and built a response to it. The response is a facility that could support people outside the CDL's provisioning system. She calls it a working prototype." I pause. "I think she has been waiting for someone to arrive with documentation of the pattern, so she would know she wasn't wrong about it." Sera sets her cup down. She does not say anything for a moment. Outside the window the city is running its morning: transit sounds, the particular quality of the atmosphere three hours into its daily tuning cycle, the low operational register of a system performing as specified. "You missed the NI sync," she says. "On the way back." "When?" "Midnight, approximately. The lock was on the second cycle when I came in." She is quiet. She is doing the same arithmetic I did before I left and again in the dead zone on the way back and again last night standing in the amber light in the hall. She is a faster calculator than I am on behavioral implications. She does not need me to walk her through what the next degradation tier means for the household registration or for the provisioning classification or for the 7-F flag's status in the CDL compliance queue. "How long before the sync degradation affects the household flag," she says. "I don't have the exact figure. The compliance office would have the current protocol. Two to four weeks, probably, before the household provisioning record reflects the updated NI status." She nods. This is the fourth answer that has technically answered her question and made the situation more serious rather than less. I watch her hold the four answers together. She has a professional vocabulary for what she is looking at. The behavioral profile of a household containing an NI at secondary degradation, four existing compliance records, a terminated institutional affiliation, and a documented relationship with an off-CDL facility in an unregistered dead zone. She knows that vocabulary better than I do. She built parts of the model that generates it. "Tell me about the facility," she says. "Physically." I describe it. The building dimensions and the roof condition and the state of the oxidized cladding. The soil beds, three inside, one along the south wall. The water system, its single weak point the valve seal three months past service. The seed storage in the northwest room, the temperature stability there, the germination trial data. The copper-wire intercom. The outbuilding to the north. Wren's three years of documented failure modes worked around and built past. Sera listens to all of it. She is not taking notes. Her hands are around her cup, very still. When I finish she says nothing for a moment. The refrigerator hums. On the wall above it, the drawing: the house, the very large garden in careful parallel lines, the sun in its conventional position. She says: "I need to tell you something." I wait. "I've been thinking about this for two weeks," she says. "Since the reclassification notice. I've been building an assessment. A professional assessment, because that's the tool I have and I was trying to use it correctly." She pauses. The pause is deliberate, not a search for words. She has the words already. "I am not saying you've had a breakdown. I am not saying the evidence isn't real. The evidence is real and you know how to read it and I know how to read the behavioral profile of a person in a crisis, and you are not presenting as someone who has lost functional assessment capacity." Another pause. "What I am presenting as, from a professional standpoint, is someone who has failed to maintain household stability. Someone whose NI is now at secondary degradation in a household that already has a 7-F reclassification flag. Someone who has an active relationship with an unregistered off-CDL facility." She stops. She looks at me. "I understand the information you've found," she says. "I understand what you're proposing. And I have a daughter who is eight years old and has been doing her homework under these charts for three months." Her voice does not change register. This is not cruelty. This is the most careful sentence she knows how to build. "The behavioral profile of this household, right now, is above the threshold I can manage responsibly while she is in it. PACEM is monitoring this household. You know the flags that are active and you know what another degradation event looks like in the system. I have a professional obligation to my own assessment, and my assessment is that I cannot protect her here." I look at her. She is looking at me. Her face is doing what it does when she has reached the end of a clinical process and has arrived at a finding she did not want. The eyes are the thing. She has not managed them. "You've been building this for longer than two weeks," I say. "Yes." The word falls into the room and neither of us does anything with it. "Where will you go," I say. "My mother's. For now." She stands. Her cup is still on the table. "I filed the separation paperwork yesterday. You'll receive a notification. The language is standard — it's a legal separation, not a dissolution. There's a difference in the CDL record." "I know the difference." She nods. She picks up the cup and sets it in the dish rack. She turns. Her posture is what it always is. Upright, contained, not closed. She is not walking away. She has finished a process. "I don't want you to think I believe you're wrong," she says. "I need you to understand that's not what this is." "I know what it is," I say. She goes. Our daughter comes out of her room twenty minutes later with her school bag on one shoulder and her coat in her hand. She is wearing the blue sweater with the small yellow pattern at the cuff that she picked herself at the provisioning exchange last month and has been wearing until the point of my asking her to change. Her hair is in two braids because Sera did it this morning and she is not yet old enough to do it herself. She stands in the kitchen doorway for a moment and I can see that she already knows. Children understand rooms. She has been in this apartment for three months of charts and NI degradation events and the particular quality of two people who are not fighting but are very careful with each other, and she is eight, which means she does not know the names for any of it and knows all of it anyway. She comes to me and she puts her hand in mine. Her hand is small and warm and weighs a specific amount that I will not be able to describe accurately later because accurate description requires a unit of measure and no unit of measure I have access to is the right one. She is holding my hand and looking up at me. She has Sera's eyes. They are a dark gray that reads as almost brown in low light, and right now the kitchen is in its morning cast, west-facing, flat, and her eyes are as gray as they get. She has a small crescent scar on her left cheekbone from a fall in the second year, mostly faded, still visible when the light is at this angle. Her face is very still. She says: "I know we're leaving." "Yes." "Are you coming?" "Not today." She is looking at me. She is too young to understand what she is looking at and old enough to understand that it is real. She is performing the gravity she can feel is required. She is doing this seriously, the way she does everything seriously. She squeezes my hand. Not urgently. The specific pressure of a child saying something she does not have words for yet. Then she lets go. She straightens her bag on her shoulder. She goes to stand by the door where Sera is waiting with their two cases. She does not look back at me. I watch her go, the blue sweater with the yellow cuff detail, the two braids, the specific weight of her hand still present in mine, the sensation of it taking a moment to dissipate the way temperature takes a moment to dissipate after the source has been removed. The door closes. I sit at the kitchen table. The apartment is exactly as it was. The chart covers most of the table, three colors of ink, the red boundary extended twice in two weeks, the perimeter still larger than the interior. The coffee cup that Sera used is in the dish rack, rinsed, upside down, its shadow on the steel. The paper chart curls at the far corner where the tape has given up. The folded page is on the shelf in the other room, between chapter four and chapter five of a book whose title I have memorized. The entry lock on the door is at secondary degradation, two cycles to resolve. The refrigerator hums. The west-facing windows hold the flat morning light that will not change until early afternoon when the atmospheric system shifts to its midday tuning. The drawing is on the refrigerator. The house with the very large garden. The sun in the upper right corner, where suns go in drawings, where she put it this morning before breakfast, the green crayon rows careful and parallel. I look at it. I do not know what she meant by it. I do not know if she meant anything by it, the way children do not mean things the way adults mean things, the way the gap between what she drew and what I am thinking about when I look at it is not a gap I can close by reasoning toward it. A house. A garden in careful rows. She has been doing homework under these charts for three months. She is eight. The coffee cup in the dish rack. The chart on the table. The folded page on the shelf. The entry lock at the second tier. The flat light in the west-facing windows. The hum of the refrigerator. The two braids and the blue sweater and the yellow cuff detail. The specific crescent of the scar at the angle where it was still visible. The weight of her hand, which I cannot measure accurately and will think about again, later, more than once. The drawing on the refrigerator. The very large garden. The house. Everything as it was. Exactly as it was this morning. Nothing has moved.

2.body: Faith operates quietly — through preserved objects, through choices made without witness, through the question Caleb cannot stop asking: whether what he is doing matters if no one ever knows. The novel's answer is provisional and earned, not preached.
2.title: About this novel

20.body: The notification arrives at 6:03 a.m. through the bone-conduction channel of my NI, which means it arrives as a voice in the structure of my skull rather than a sound in the room. I am standing at the west-facing window watching the automated transit corridor twelve floors below, where the first freight vehicles of the morning are moving through their sequenced channels, and the voice speaks my name in the specific tone of PACEM's public communication system, the one I helped calibrate in the third year of the deployment pilot, the timbre we chose because it tested in the forty-second percentile for compliance response and the thirty-first percentile for anxiety induction. We wanted compliance without distress. We did not want the system to seem threatening. The research consensus was that a neutral, professional timbre would be perceived as authoritative without being coercive. I remember the discussion. I was in the room. I had an opinion about the frequency distribution in the upper register, and we implemented it, and now the voice of that work is in my skull at 6:03 a.m. speaking my name. The message is forty-one words: This is an automated notification from PACEM Provisioning Services. Your Neural Interface has missed two synchronization cycles. Your provisioning authorization has been temporarily adjusted. Please visit a regional compliance office to receive your updated classification. Thank you. The word "Thank you" sits in my skull for a moment after the message ends. I had not seen that particular sign-off in the documentation I worked on. It was added later, I think. User testing, probably. I hold the message in my head the way I hold technical specifications. Forty-one words. Two missed sync cycles. Provisioning authorization temporarily adjusted. I look at the transit corridor below. The freight vehicles move through their channels with the specific economy of a system with nothing to prove, no audience to perform for, no waste in its motion. Below the corridor level, one of the atmospheric station arrays runs its 6 a.m. calibration cycle. The air quality reading for this sector will update in twelve minutes, and the city's atmospheric management layer will log it, and no one will review the log because the system is performing as specified and there is nothing to review. The kiosk in the ground-floor lobby shows a red indicator before I have finished removing my gloves. The screen does not explain itself beyond a referral number and a printed slip, which the machine produces with the same unhurried pace it uses for everything. I fold the slip into my coat pocket. The referral is to the compliance office in the Threshold East transit hub, eight minutes on foot. I go. The transit hub is a building I passed through most mornings, before the Nexus Corp suspension and the weeks of working from the apartment and the hours at the kitchen table with the chart. It is a functional space: high ceilings for the drone mesh, automated boarding zones along the east wall, the infrastructure of a system that processes a hundred thousand individual movements a day and has optimized that processing to the point where it no longer requires the transit hub to appear as anything other than what it is. Efficient, unremarkable, doing its job. The compliance office is on the west side of the hub, between a provisioning kiosk and a transit information station. The door is unmarked except for a number and a small PACEM Compliance Services placard. I go in. There is a waiting area with four chairs, institutional furniture in a gray-beige composite I recognize as the standard for public-service interiors across Vael's transit infrastructure. Two chairs are occupied. A man who is older than me, sitting very still, holding a referral slip with both hands. A woman who looks to be in her mid-twenties, coat buttoned, watching the numbered display above the service counter with an attention that suggests she has been waiting long enough to be ready for it to change. I sit. It changes in four minutes. The older man goes up. The woman follows three minutes after that. I am called six minutes after she returns to retrieve her coat. The officer is a young man, mid-twenties, in the standard compliance services uniform: gray, close-fitting, the PACEM emblem on the left chest. He is sitting in front of a workstation that occupies most of his desk, and he is looking at his screen when I approach the counter, and then he looks at me. His face has the professional quality of someone who has done this enough times that his face no longer requires management. Not cold. Not warm. Simply the face you keep in place when you are processing a case. "Name and NI number," he says. I give them. He looks at his screen. He uses a stylus to navigate something I cannot see from my side of the counter. He says: "Two missed sync cycles. Seven-F reclassification flag, nine months active. Terminated institutional affiliation, five months. Current compliance record: four entries." He says all of this in the order it appears in the record, not in the order of severity or meaning. He is reading the record. He is not interpreting it. "Your provisioning tier has been adjusted to Level Three pending NI resync. Level Three covers base caloric allocation, municipal utilities, and transit access. It does not cover supplemental provisions, elective medical services, or household upgrade categories." He says all of this the way he would explain a transit schedule: these stops, this route, this time. The tone is exactly what it is designed to be. "The restart window is nine days," he says. "If your NI completes a sync event by that date, your provisioning tier will restore to its prior classification pending a standard compliance review. If your NI does not complete a sync event within the nine-day window, your account will be flagged for Restricted classification, which triggers full provisioning denial, housing non-compliance designation, and a physical assessment referral." He looks at me. His eyes make contact without pressure, without particular attention. He is confirming I have heard and understood the information. "Do you have questions about the timeline?" "No," I say. He taps something on his stylus. He says: "Your updated authorization will be active in your NI within the hour. The nine-day window begins today." He slides a printed summary across the counter. "Keep this for your records." I pick it up. He is already looking at his screen. I say: "Thank you." I do not know why I say it. He nods, once, and I go. The transit hub outside the compliance office is doing what it does at 7:40 a.m. on a late-December morning: moving people. The automated boarding zones on the east wall are running their first shift peak, and the crowd has the specific quality of people in their habitual routes, people who know what they are doing and are doing it without having to decide. I stand for a moment near the corridor junction south of the compliance office door and let the motion pass around me. Above, in the upper tier of the hub's ceiling structure, a drone from the standard mesh passes on its routing loop. It is one of the midsize units, the M-series, which I know because I reviewed the hardware specification for the transit hub mesh deployment seven years ago and the M-series was the unit selected for indoor hub coverage. Forty-meter range. Four-channel optical array. On-device processing for movement pattern recognition, facial geometry, behavioral flag scoring. It is not watching me. It is watching everything, which is a distinction I understand precisely. I know, from the inside, what my current threat level costs the system. A single individual at a 7-F reclassification, provisioning Level Three, no active institutional affiliation, no current organizational contact pattern that would flag as coordinated dissent. The system has allocated bandwidth to my account, but bandwidth is not the same as attention. My account is in a queue. The queue is not long enough to be notable; it is precisely long enough that the automated scoring has placed me in the next tier of passive monitoring, which means my NI queries are logged with a higher retention period and my location data is flagged for review if it matches a list of coordinates associated with known compliance cases. I know something else about bandwidth assessment. You can be completely accurate about your current threat level and still not account for the moment when the queue processes. I know this from the documentation, from the cases I found. The twenty-one people in my chart were not high-threat-level actors. They were individuals at flag status who had tried to move information through channels. The queue had processed them, at the pace the system runs, and the pattern had been the same each time. You are not that interesting, until you are. The threshold is not announced. I fold the printed summary and put it with the referral slip in my coat pocket. I go out through the east transit exit into the cold December air and I walk home. Nine days, operationally, is the following set of conditions. If I sync before the deadline: the NI degradation reverses. My provisioning tier restores to its prior classification pending review, which will add to my compliance record but will not automatically result in further restriction. The authentication degradation stops advancing, which means the CDL access I still have through the home terminal stabilizes at its current state. The terminal's CDL access has been running through my NI authentication; with two missed syncs the access layer has been restricted to read-only on most data categories. It did not lose access entirely because the read-only tier is a standard degraded state rather than a suspension, but the difference between read-only and full access is the difference between observing the record and being able to document changes in it. That capacity is already reduced. If I sync, I also return to the surveillance baseline from which the past four months are visible and searchable. My documented cases are stored in fragmented form across the local terminal and the paper chart and the single folded page in the paperback on the office shelf. The paper and the folded page are not in the system. The terminal documentation is on a local drive that the CDL's automated audit process would require a directed compliance review to access, not a passive sweep. If I do not sync: Restricted classification in nine days. Full provisioning denial means no food access through any managed kiosk in Vael. Housing non-compliance designation means the building authority receives a flag on my apartment, which begins a remediation process that results in a supervised inspection within thirty days and a housing displacement order within sixty. The physical assessment referral triggers a compliance officer visit. What happens after that visit depends on the assessment outcome, and the documentation on assessment outcomes is not public. I have Wren. I have the facility. I have the chart on the kitchen table and the folded page in the paperback and twenty-one documented cases in my head and on the local drive. I have the knowledge in my head. I have nine days. I walk. The cold in the transit corridors is the managed kind, temperature held within the standard comfort range, not the real cold that starts forty minutes outside Vael's boundary. The people I pass are doing what they do in the morning: moving with purpose through space that exists to move them. I am moving in the same direction as most of them, which is home, and I am carrying a printed summary in my coat pocket, and I am doing what I have been doing for three months, which is counting what I have. The apartment is exactly as I left it. The chart covers most of the kitchen table, three colors of ink, the red boundary extended twice since October. The coffee cup is in the dish rack, rinsed from this morning. The west-facing windows hold the flat December light at an angle that will not change until the atmospheric system shifts at midday. The folded page is on the shelf in the other room, between chapter four and chapter five. Sera's things are gone. The closet in the bedroom is empty on one side. Her workbag is not on the hook by the door. The glass she kept on the bathroom shelf, the specific blue glass from the provisioning exchange two years ago, is not there. None of this is a surprise. I watched her take these things. I know exactly which objects are absent. I notice them because I notice everything more precisely when I am afraid, and I have been afraid since the entry lock took two cycles in October, and I am still afraid, and the precise inventory of what is no longer here is the thing my eyes produce when I walk through the apartment. The drawing is on the refrigerator. I have not taken it down. The house with the very large garden in careful parallel rows of green crayon, the sun in the upper right corner, the brown walls. She drew it the morning they left. I do not know what she meant by it. She is eight. I sit at the kitchen table with the printed summary. Level Three provisioning. Nine-day restart window. I set it down next to the chart. Three months of this. Before the three months: fifteen years of the work I did and the report I buried and the anomaly I let Sone tell me was noise. After three months: four compliance records. No institutional affiliation. The 7-F flag marks me in every system query in Vael. Sera's things are gone. The entry lock takes two cycles. I am on Level Three provisioning, which means every kiosk in the city now shows a red indicator before I have touched the screen. I have sent the evidence to six people who might have acted on it. I have been told three times that my findings are unverified and my conduct since the reclassification has generated concerns about my professional judgment. I have been told once that the organization I contacted has no capacity to take on new cases of this type. The remaining two have not responded. I found Wren, who is one person in a building forty minutes outside Vael with a water system and a seed store. I have the evidence. I have the cases. I have everything I had three months ago plus a clearer picture of what having it has cost. I look at the chart. The red boundary. The cases that cluster in the healthcare allocation module, the same module where Wren's patients lost their prioritization scores. The twenty-one names, which are not really names, they are referral codes, initials, the shorthand I developed for keeping the documentation in a form that was harder to read if someone found it before I understood who would find it first. I have been trying to be believed for three months. I have not been believed by anyone who could act on it. I sit with this for a while. The refrigerator hums. The light moves in the west-facing windows as the atmospheric system runs its midday calibration. The drawing is on the refrigerator. The very large garden. The house. The sun in the corner where suns go. Nine days. I look at the chart and I think about what it has been for: documentation to support a case I was trying to make to someone who would verify it and act on it. Evidence organized for an audience. The chart is a communication, aimed outward, at a recipient I have not been able to reach. Three months of it aimed at someone who would receive it. And then I am thinking something I have not thought before, and the thought is not finished, it is only the beginning of a question I do not have the full shape of yet, but here is the shape it is starting to take: What would it mean to stop trying to be believed? I do not answer it. I do not know the answer. I sit at the table with the chart and the printed summary and the drawing on the refrigerator and the question in my head, and the apartment is quiet, and no one answers it for me.

22.body: By the time my NI moved from Restricted pending to full Restricted, I had already stopped sleeping through the sync window. The third miss triggered the standard ladder: kiosk refusals, out-of-residence authentication failures, transit friction, then provisioning non-availability, what people inside Nexus used to call hard eject when the door was closed. The language is careful. The outcome is not. You eat where you planned ahead, or where someone already outside the system planned ahead for you. I made three trips to Verdant after the reclassification. Night routes only. Old maintenance links that disappeared from current maps after corridor expansion and mesh regularization. Wren gave me her sweep table for the rural boundary: three years of dates, cloud cover, vehicle noise, and the kind of silence that means one unit has passed and the next has not started yet. Accurate data. Field data. Never perfect. On the fourth trip, I leave the apartment at 2:11 a.m. The hall lights in Threshold East are at low-power night settings. The entry lock resolves on the first cycle because I am still authenticated inside registered residence. Outside, the air has the dry metallic edge of reduced overnight particulate suppression. My bicycle chain ticks once on the first full pedal turn and then settles. I count six full mesh passes between the north corridor and the old service route at Sector Twelve. I wait through two behind a concrete barrier that used to shield a distribution yard and now shields gravel, weeds, and a rusted sign worn down to matte gray. Forty-three minutes from the boundary marker, the managed surface ends and the old agricultural cut begins. The temperature drops all at once. Ground cold. Drainage cold. The cold that sits in low places and stays there. My gloves are thin at the fingertips from two weeks of night riding. The brake lever bites into that thin place. I can feel the metal shape through the fabric. I kill my light before the final rise and walk the bicycle the last three hundred meters. No engine noise. No voice traffic. No rotor note. One dog, far east, then quiet. Verdant sits low from this angle, dark except for one panel of muted green light where the lowest grow setting keeps winter trays alive. I do not call out. We do not use names outside walls. The cache is in the cistern annex, in the old concrete chamber where Wren's first filtration array failed before she replaced it with the current gravity sequence. We use it when one of us is on a route the other cannot risk that night. The chamber smells of iron, wet stone, and old algae caught in seams no brush reaches. I kneel, unseat the second panel, and reach into the dry section behind the intake pipe. My fingers touch canvas first, then paper. For a moment I think it is one of Wren's notebooks wrapped for moisture, then I pull it clear and the shape is wrong. Too thick at the spine, too narrow in height, sewn signature bulge visible through the oilcloth. Heavier than expected for its size. I know what it is before I unwrap it. There are not many objects built this way. The wrapper is two layers, oilcloth over waxed paper, tied with ordinary twine in a knot you can open without cutting. I untie it with gloves on, then remove one glove because I cannot feel enough through the fabric. The cover is dark, nearly black, no jacket, edges worn pale where hands have taken it on and off shelves for years. The spine has been repaired once with cloth tape the color of old bone. The title is embossed and almost gone. Holy Bible. I stand in the chamber with the book in my hand and do nothing for longer than I intend. Water moves through the active pipe to my left with the low continuous sound of pressure and stone. The floor is cold through my soles. I have not held a Bible in twenty years. I have seen scripture as archived text blocks and digitized fragments and obituary quotations before moderation removes them. Not this. This is an object meant to be opened. I rewrap the oilcloth loosely, keep the book under my coat, and go inside. The main room is in night configuration. Three overhead fixtures off, one at minimum. Grow banks along the interior wall, each strip running low enough to hold seedlings in maintenance without draining battery reserve. The air has two temperatures: cold above shoulder height, warm near the tables where compost heat and ballast collect. Humidity carries damp soil, copper from old intercom coils, yeast from yesterday's bread rack. Wren's boots are by the inner door. She is here. I hear no movement. I set the book on the steel worktable under the weakest light that still allows reading. The table has knife marks, wrench slips, and one burn where a soldering iron rolled and lay for six seconds before someone moved it. There is an empty cup with a dry tea ring at the base. A page clipped behind the sink shows Wren's valve rotation schedule with tomorrow already filled in. She did not leave this book here. Someone else reached our compartment. I look back toward the annex door. If someone can place an object in the cache, someone can map our exchange rhythm. I check the panel seam in my head, the scrape pattern on the floor, the hook point where the twine caught when I pulled it free. No forced entry. No sign of a rushed hand. No marker I can trust. I sit. My knees complain when I bend past ninety degrees, the stiffness from repeated cold exposure and too little recovery. I hold my palms around the tin cup even though it is empty. The metal warms slowly. I open the book. The first pages I see are not the beginning. Maps, then thin columns of names, then a header with the top corner torn away. I turn forward, then back, then stop and let the pages fall where wear wants them to fall. The book opens near the middle of the first quarter. Genesis 6. I read standing, then sit again because my balance feels wrong. The words are familiar in shape before they are familiar in meaning. I remember my grandfather's kitchen table, pine top worn smooth where elbows polished it over decades, his thumb flattening a page while he read aloud in a voice like gravel and summer dust. He tapped lines with the knuckle of his free hand when he wanted to mark structure. Specific instruction, he used to say, before reading a number. He liked numbers in scripture. Said you could test a person by what they did with numbers. I read the passage once through. Corruption. Violence. Decision. Then instructions: make an ark of gopher wood, rooms, covering, length, breadth, height, window, door, levels. Three hundred cubits. Fifty. Thirty. I read it again. The first read for me is architectural. The dimensions are presented as work. Material class, envelope treatment, structural sequence. Seal it inside and outside with pitch. Place the door in the side. Build three levels. Enough detail to begin. Not enough detail to remove judgment. I put my index finger on the dimension line and calculate rough scale from memory. Cubit conversion varies by source, but the ratio holds. Long enough for distribution. Narrow enough for integrity in moving water. Height for deck separation and air volume. Not random. Not poetic. Work. The south water line knocks once and settles. A hinge moves in the inner room, then stillness again. My hands are on the page and I realize I have tightened them enough to crease the margin. I release pressure and smooth the paper with the side of my palm. I have spent fifteen years tracing outputs to inputs. The phrase in my reports was input chain. In a valid system, the chain terminates in a source you can name, test, question. In PACEM's audit deception, it terminates in null. Here, the source is named. Instruction first. Action second. Build this, then enter it. I close my eyes and the room does not go dark. The grow lights are visible through my eyelids, a diffuse green-red field that shifts when I move my head. My back hurts from the ride and from a month of sleeping in positions that keep one ear available for the hall lock, stairwell, and any purposeful footsteps after midnight. My jaw is tight. I unclench it. The muscle jumps once. At my kitchen table in Vael I asked: What would it mean to stop trying to be believed. I had treated that as an audience question. If not Sone, then who. If not archive contacts, then who. If not formal channels, then who can receive evidence and act. Wrong variable. Not who will believe. What to build before belief matters. I open my notebook. It is the same one I have carried since October: weatherproof cover, graph paper, half the pages filled with route times, lock-cycle observations, provisioning counts, battery draw estimates, rough material lists from the months when query flags still mattered to me in a different way. The last page holds numbers from two nights ago, pump output versus barrel loss at near-freeze temperature. My cold-weather handwriting narrows and leans right as the hand stiffens. I turn to the next blank page. I start to write what I always write: objectives, dependencies, constraints. I stop. I write one word: Build. The pen catches at the end of the d where the paper fibers rise. I press down again and darken the line until the word is almost black compared to the rest of the page. There is no list under it. No numbered items. Not confidence. Not certainty. Sequence. One action before the others. I sit with the notebook open and the book beside it and listen to the building. The inverter hum. Water moving through the gravity line as the tank equalizes. A branch on the north side touching sheet metal each time wind pushes. Two small clicks from the heating relay cycling to keep minimum tray temperature on the east wall. The room has the quiet of a place kept alive by work that does not stop because no one is watching. At 5:02 a.m. by my watch, first light starts to unseal the top edge of the east windows. The interior is still dark, but the dark has direction. I stand, close the notebook, and read the passage once more, slower. I do not search for argument against it. I do not search for emotional confirmation. I read the dimensions again. Length. Breadth. Height. I close the book. For a moment I hold it the way my grandfather held his: palm under the spine, thumb on the page edge, weight centered so the object does not slide. My hand adjusts before I decide to adjust it. He used to pause like this before setting it down at the end of morning reading, making sure the page he wanted would open easily the next day. I do not know who put this book in the cistern annex. I do not know if it was meant for me or for whoever reached into that compartment first. I do not know if there is another note I have not found. I do not know if Wren knows more and chose not to say. I know someone carried a physical Bible across monitored ground and placed it where our hands would meet in the dark. I slide it inside my coat, against the inner layer where body heat will keep the paper above the cold point on the ride back. The cover presses flat against my chest. I can feel the corners when I breathe. Outside, the sky over the eastern fields lifts from black to iron gray. The route back to Vael is still possible for another hour before traffic and light narrow the options. I pick up my gloves, my notebook, and the wrench I forgot to return to the pegboard last week. I set the wrench back where it belongs. Then I step into the morning with the book under my coat and the word in my notebook and no plan beyond it.

24.body: Wren points at the tank mount before she says good morning. The mount sits on the north wall of the former processing bay, above shoulder height, a steel frame bolted into poured concrete that has hairline fractures from freeze and thaw cycles older than either of us. One bolt on the lower right bracket has split through the shank and pulled two millimeters away from the washer. The load is still held, but only because the left side is taking weight it was not designed to take. "That one fails first," she says. She taps the cracked bolt head with a wrench. The ring is higher than a sound bolt. "Re-brace before we add the second barrel." She turns the wrench in her hand and holds it out to me. I take it. The handle is cold enough that the skin at my palm sticks for a moment before grip heat resolves it. It is a standard box-end, seventeen millimeter, steel worn smooth where years of use have removed the factory texture. My hand does not know this weight. It has known keyboards, stylus pens, glass touch surfaces, paper. Not this. Wren has already moved to the ladder by the time I look up. She plants one boot on the first rung and tests it with a short bounce. "You staying through dusk, or riding back?" I put the wrench in my coat pocket and take off my gloves. The dawn cold in this room has two layers, concrete cold at shin height and metal cold at hand height. "I am staying," I say. She nods once, like a person acknowledging a measurement that is now confirmed, and climbs. "Then we work the order right. Tank, line, soil. The rest when there is time." The Bible is inside my coat against my ribs. The single folded page is inside the paperback in my bag under the worktable. The notebook with one word written in dark pressure is in my left pocket. Build. I follow her up the ladder. The bracket failure is visible at close range. The crack in the bolt is not fresh, which means it has held through multiple load cycles and then started to propagate after the temperature drop this month. I put the box end on the nut and test torque with light pressure. It gives too easily, then catches on deformation in the thread. Wren passes me a clamp from below, then a short segment of angle iron with two drilled holes. "We sister the load," she says. "Do not trust the existing anchor." "Material class?" I ask. She squints at the iron and scrapes one edge with her thumb nail. "Mild steel. Salvaged rack support from the east bay. Strong enough if we spread the force across two points." I hold the angle iron to the mount. The holes are not aligned for this bracket. "We need a third hole." "We need a fourth as well," she says, and climbs down for the hand drill. The drill is manual, brace type, with a crank and a chuck that closes by key. I have seen one in archive photographs. I have not used one. Wren tightens the bit, sets the point where I marked with a pencil line, and rotates the crank with short hard turns until the bit bites. Then she hands it up to me. "Keep pressure vertical," she says. "If you walk the bit sideways you oval the hole and it will chatter loose by January." I start the cut. The first turns are smooth. At eight turns, resistance increases and the handle pushes back into my palm. At fifteen, my shoulder starts to burn in a narrow line under the right clavicle where repetitive load lives when you are not conditioned for it. At twenty-two, the bit breaks through and my wrist drops because I put too much weight into the final push. Wren catches the drill before it falls. "Less force at breakthrough," she says. She does not say anything about my hand shaking. "Second hole." We cut the second hole and seat the brace. She holds the plate flat while I drive the bolt through and thread the nut from underneath. The nut catches at halfway because I am cross-threading. She reaches up, puts her fingers over mine, backs it out a quarter turn, and reseats it on the true thread without speaking. "Now tighten," she says. "Not all the way. Alternate sides." I alternate, quarter turns left then right, until the brace draws flush against the mount and the sound of the wrench changes from free ring to dull strike. We test by hanging half our weight from the lower frame and watching for movement at the anchor. There is none. Wren leans back on the ladder and exhales through her nose. "Good. That buys us two weeks, maybe three." "Two weeks to what?" She climbs down and wipes her hands on her trouser legs. "To adding barrel two and moving the intake screen before freeze shuts the ground hard." She points toward the inner door. "Line next." By 8:10 we are at the south wall trench where the current water line enters from the collection tank outside. The line is polyethylene, older than the current valve assembly, routed too close to the upper slab where night cold reaches it before dawn. Wren has laid out the replacement section: twenty meters of newer line, two couplings, one check valve, one shutoff, one clamp kit, insulation wrap salvaged from transit HVAC ductwork. The trench is shallow and narrow. We can re-route inside without digging exterior ground, which would take half a day and announce itself in ways soil disturbances announce themselves. We shut the feed and open the relief valve. The line spits once, then dribbles for forty seconds before pressure drops. "You cut here," Wren says, kneeling by the marker line she drew in chalk. She puts the pipe cutter in my hand. "Straight cut. If you angle it, the coupling seals unevenly." I kneel. Concrete cold comes through the fabric at both knees. The pipe cutter has a ratchet head with spring return. I set it on the line and squeeze. The first bite dents but does not sever. The second bite goes through with a soft snap and a small spray of water the relief did not clear. Water hits my sleeve and runs toward my wrist. I look at the exposed end. The cut is two degrees off perpendicular. "Again," Wren says. I cut one centimeter back to square it. This time the edge is clean. We fit the coupling, seat the gasket, tighten the clamp to stop point. My fingers are clumsy on the set screw because cold has flattened sensation in the last joint of each finger. I pull my hand away, breathe into it twice, and continue. We move down the trench in one-meter sections. Cut, seat, clamp, test alignment. The sequence has no interpretation layer and no persuasion layer. There is no one to convince. There is only whether the line seals. At 9:37 the first full run is assembled and wrapped. We open the feed valve by one quarter turn. Water enters with a low rushing sound, then a higher note where it passes the new check valve. A bead forms at the third coupling and starts to travel. "Leak," I say. Wren is already there with the wrench. She tightens the clamp one eighth turn, then another. The bead stops growing. She wipes the joint with her thumb, holds her breath, waits. No second bead. "Again," she says. We repeat the run six times. Three joints hold on first attempt. Two require adjustment. One requires disassembly because the gasket folded under insertion. By the time the line stays dry under full flow, my lower back has developed the deep hot ache that starts at the spine and wraps toward the abdomen when you have been bent for hours in a posture your body does not recognize as normal. Wren stands and stretches both arms overhead until her shoulder joints click. "Eat now," she says. "Soil in forty minutes." We eat at the worktable with our coats still on. Bread from yesterday, dense and slightly sour, preserved carrots, and a cup of tea that tastes faintly of copper because the kettle sits on a coil that needs descaling. Wren tears the bread with both hands and sets half on my plate. "If you stay," she says, looking at the loaf instead of at me, "you do not do Vael runs except emergency. Your NI is past red. In-city ping puts you on active watch, then physical pickup." I hold the cup with both hands. The heat at the base reaches into my palms slowly. "I know the ladder." "Knowing is not the same as accepting." She chews, swallows, takes another piece of bread. "When your address misses compliance inspection, housing code escalates. They will classify you as non-resident or evasive depending on which bot reaches the file first. Either way, your record hardens." "It already hardened." She looks at me now. There is dirt under one nail from the trench. "Harder than this. Eliminated classification is not theory." I set the cup down because my hand is gripping too hard. The tin rings against steel. "If I return, they have me anyway. If I stay, I work until they have me." Wren nods once. "Correct." She wipes crumbs from the table into her palm and drops them in a metal bowl for the hens in the west enclosure. "Then stop naming the choice like it is still open." I keep my eyes on the ring of tea at the bottom of the cup. The ring trembles because my hand is still on the table edge and not steady yet. The room smells of wet wool, carrot brine, and iron dust from the morning drill work. I run the return sequence once, start to finish: bicycle south, service road, checkpoint gate, scan, hold room, interview. Every branch ends in the same place. I swallow and feel the dryness in my throat catch halfway. The cup has gone cold now. My shoulders are up near my ears, and I force them down. "Understood," I say. She pushes back from the table and stands. "Good. Soil." The adjacent field is east of the facility, beyond the concrete apron where transport trucks used to reverse into loading bays before integration rerouted freight through automated corridors. The field has been unmanaged for years. Dead stalks from last season's volunteer growth stand in uneven rows where wind and runoff deposited seed. The top layer is frozen crust over damp loam. Near the old drainage ditch, the ground is compacted enough that a boot heel barely marks it. Wren has marked a sampling grid with stakes and orange string. Four by six points, ten-meter spacing, plus two control points near the existing grow area for baseline comparison. She hands me a soil auger and a roll of sample bags. "Depth at fifteen centimeters and thirty," she says. She rubs at her wrist where the glove seam has chafed a line into the skin. "Label both. If you hit rubble, offset half a meter and note it." I press the auger into point A1. The first ten centimeters go easily. Below that, resistance changes from root mat to clay. I rotate the handle and lift. The core comes up striped: dark organic top, lighter brown below, gray flecks at the base from old concrete aggregate. I bag the first segment as A1-15. The second core at thirty contains more gray flecks and one rust-red shard I cannot identify. By point A4, my hands are blistering under the base of the fingers where the auger handle rubs on each turn. The skin is intact but hot and tight. I switch grip orientation. By point B2, the first blister on my right palm opens. I wrap it with tape from the med kit and continue. The wind shifts from northwest around noon. It carries colder air off the empty lots beyond the boundary trees and pushes fine grit against the side of my face. The sensation is static first, then abrasion. I pull my scarf higher and keep turning the auger. We move through the grid without much talk. Wren calls out point numbers; I repeat and bag. At C5 the auger hits metal at twelve centimeters, hollow sound, likely buried conduit from the old processing line. We offset and continue. At D3 the lower sample smells faintly sulfuric, enough to flag for treatment. By F1 both depths are dark and loose, highest organic content in the set. I realize I have started counting turns out loud to keep my grip even. "That corner first," Wren says when I read out F1. She points with her pencil toward the southwest quadrant where drainage slope is gentler. She shifts her weight off her left knee before continuing. "If we break ground there in spring, we can add three beds without changing current irrigation pressure." "How many calories at full yield?" She pulls a folded chart from her pocket and smooths it against the sample crate. "With current seed stock and weather averages, twelve hundred extra kilocalories per day in peak season, six hundred shoulder season, two hundred winter if we preserve right and nothing fails." She looks up. "Nothing fails is not a realistic variable." "No." "Write with failure included." I write it, and by 14:30 we have forty-eight sample bags labeled and stacked in two crates. We carry them inside and set them by the sink for pH and nitrate strip testing. The strips are old stock, expired six months. We run control tests against known soil from current beds and adjust interpretation drift before logging the field points. The first set reads high acid at A2 and B4, moderate acid elsewhere, near neutral at F1 and F2. Nitrate is low across the top row and better in the lower two. I start a table on graph paper, then add columns as outliers stack: contamination flags, remediation hours, compost volume, labor at two workers and at three. Halfway down the second page my pen slips because my taped palm is wet from melted grit. Wren watches me for a minute, scraping mud from her boots with a flathead screwdriver. "You make spreadsheets in your head," she says. "I make failure forecasts." "Same thing if you are pessimistic enough." She bangs the boots twice against the threshold to dislodge packed clay and returns in socks. "Do one more table. Water draw increase if we add those beds." "I need pump performance at current temperature." She points toward the panel by the sink where she has recorded daily pump readings in pencil for three years. "You have it." Her finger leaves a clean line in dust on the edge of the board. I walk to the panel. The sheet is full to the edge, dates in a narrow hand, morning and evening readings, notes when values drifted: seal wear, intake clog, relay lag. Three years of data in graphite and paper tape. No cloud backup. No query interface. No abstraction layer between event and record. At 15:10 we check the tank mount again. The new brace holds. At 15:32 we run full flow through the re-routed line and inspect each coupling dry. At 16:05 we move the second empty barrel into position below the mount but do not add water. Wren marks tomorrow's task list on the wall slate: mount reinforcement pass two, intake screen cleaning, compost turn, relay inspection. I take my bag to the former seed storage room that is now my bunk. The room is narrow, concrete walls, one small window taped with translucent plastic to reduce heat loss, one cot with two blankets, one crate for personal items. I put the Bible in the crate, wrapped again in oilcloth, and set my notebook on top of it. The folded page stays in the paperback in my coat pocket until I decide where it can live without becoming easy to lose. When I pull my gloves off, I see the beginning of dirt lines in the creases of my fingers that soap will not fully remove tonight. There is dried blood at the edge of the tape on my right palm where the blister opened. I peel the tape slowly. The skin beneath is raw, half-moon shaped, painful when exposed to air. I wash at the utility sink. Water is cold enough to ache at first contact. Soil runs off in brown threads. The metal smell from the pipes remains on my fingers even after scrubbing with lye soap. I dry my hands on a towel that has been washed too many times and stiffened at the edges. By dusk the temperature has dropped again. We close the outer vents to the night setting and check latch points on both service doors. Wren walks the perimeter with a headlamp set to red filter. I follow with the clipboard and note points that need repair: west fence section loose at post three, greenhouse panel crack widening on upper seam, one hinge on the south gate binding under cold contraction. When we finish, she stands at the threshold and rolls her neck once to release stiffness. "You did not drop anything important," she says. "I dropped the drill." "I said important." She reaches past me, takes the clipboard, and hangs it on the hook by the door. "Eat, then write what you saw. If it stays in your head, you lose half by morning." We eat lentils and root vegetables from a pot that has been simmering since afternoon. No one speaks for several minutes. The room sounds are enough: spoon against bowl, low hum of inverter, one short cluck from the hens settling in the enclosure. My forearms are heavy from repetitive load. Each time I lift the spoon, the tendons at my wrist pull over inflamed tissue and complain. After we clean the bowls, I go outside with the notebook and sit on an overturned crate facing the east field. Light is almost gone. The horizon holds a thin strip of steel gray above black ground. The facility behind me gives off a low warm glow through patched windows. I turn to a blank page and start the list. Water system: secondary barrel installation, intake screen redesign, redundancy for check valve, winter insulation over exposed line, replacement gasket stock, manual pump backup. Power: battery capacity margin at current draw, relay replacement for heater circuit, spare inverter board, fuel reserve for emergency generator even though we avoid running it, insulation repair at east wall to reduce overnight loss. I flex my right hand and feel the tape pull across raw skin. Food production: spring field remediation southwest quadrant first, compost volume expansion, seed viability testing by lot, preservation schedule, rodent-proof storage bins, calorie model by season with failure ranges. Shelter: bunk expansion if third and fourth occupants arrive, weather sealing for north door, mold inspection in lower annex, spare blankets, clothing repair station. A draft comes through the gap at the service door and cools the sweat at the base of my neck. Medical: inventory antibiotics by expiration, suture stock, analgesic ration plan, splint materials, infection protocols when water pressure is low, documentation for triage if arrivals exceed capacity. Security and movement: route table updates by week, dead drop protocol revisions, light discipline chart, noise discipline after dusk, fallback points if facility compromised, burn plan for sensitive notes, signal system for approach without names. Work allocation: daily minimum tasks, weekly maintenance rotation, contingency if one person injured, training sequence for anyone new so failures do not cascade through ignorance. Governance: decision protocol when two disagree, allocation protocol when resources tighten, recordkeeping standard that survives if one of us is taken. The pen scratches slower as my fingers stiffen in the cold, but the list keeps growing. Every item generates two more items when traced to dependency. Add beds requires water. Add water requires mounts and seals. Mounts and seals require materials we do not yet have and routes we may not be able to run once patrol density changes. More people require food. More food requires soil correction and labor hours we only have if one of us is not sick and not hiding and not gone. By the time I stop, my hand is cramping along the thumb pad. I flex it and watch the tendon move under skin that is already rougher than it was this morning. The page is full front and back. I turn it and continue on the next page, then the next. When I finally look up, Wren is standing in the doorway with one hand on the frame, waiting without calling my name. "You coming in?" she asks. "In a minute," I say. She studies my face for a moment, then nods. "Do not stay out long. Temperature drops hard after full dark." She goes back inside and lets the door close most of the way, not latched, leaving a thin bar of light across the dirt. I read the list once from top to bottom. It is not a plan. It is an inventory of requirements for a thing that does not exist yet and may not exist in time. The first line in this notebook is still one word. Build. Everything after it is consequence. I put the pen down and hold my hands out in the last light. Dirt is worked into the skin along the cuticles and around the new tape on my palm. The callus has not formed. The pain has. This is a very long list for a man who started today, and the system that erased me in Vael has already started counting how long I have been gone.
