26.body: At 5:03 a.m., the line feeding bed three goes soft under my thumb and then firms again when I lift the valve arm another five degrees. The pressure fluctuation is brief, less than two seconds, but long enough to mark. I run the check again from bed one, palm on each line in sequence, eyes on the clear segment near the manifold where microbubbles show turbulence before a gauge notices it. Bed one stable. Bed two stable. Bed three one brief drop, then steady. Bed four newly added, the extension we finished four days ago, stable at low flow and still within the draw range I wrote on the wall chart. The floor is cold through both sock layers. My breath condenses against the underside of the poly cover above the seedlings and returns as water drops at irregular intervals. The drop pattern has shifted in the last week because we changed the angle of the support bars to open six more trays along the west side. Thirty percent more grow surface, measured twice and recalculated when the first number felt optimistic. The extension works. It also increases morning labor by forty-three minutes when both of us are healthy and moving at baseline. My right palm no longer needs tape. The blister from the auger split in chaptered layers, hardened, and finally became callus thick enough that the shovel handle no longer raises heat after ten minutes. The callus is not uniform. There is a ridge where skin healed unevenly. At night it catches on the wool blanket if I forget to flex my hand before sleep. I close the valve arm and listen. Water enters the trays with the low channel sound that means the slope is holding. Behind me, the inverter hum stays flat. No relay click. No pump cavitation. Outside, wind moves through the corrugated siding with the dry-paper sound we get when temperature drops below freezing and the panel seams contract. Wren comes in from the yard carrying two empty crates and sets them down with her hip because both hands are gloved and wet. "You have the bed chart?" she asks. I hand her the clipboard. "Pressure dip on three. Brief. Corrected with valve increase." She scans the line I added, then nods. "Write the outside temperature. If it does it again tonight we insulate the elbow at the corner, not the manifold." She pulls one glove off with her teeth, tucks it under her arm, and writes a note under mine in the tight script she uses for anything that might matter later. Then she puts the glove back on and hooks her thumb toward the north wall. "Mount check before breakfast," she says. "If the upper plate shifted, barrel two comes down today and I do not want it to." The north-wall mount no longer has the temporary sister brace we installed my first day. Three weeks after that repair, we stripped the old anchor hardware, drilled six new points into uncracked concrete, and seated a doubled bracket with backing plates cut from pallet rack steel. I held the light while Wren read the wall for hidden fractures by sound, tapping each area with the blunt end of a chisel and listening for the hollow ring that means failure is waiting behind the surface. We hoisted barrel two with a rope block rigged through the old beam channel and filled it in quarter stages to watch the frame under load. It held. It is still holding. "If it shifted, it shifted at the lower left," I say. "The right side is cleaner than yesterday." Wren snorts once, almost a laugh. "You sound like a man who has spent six weeks staring at bolts." "That is accurate." "Good," she says. "We need one." We eat standing at the worktable because both of us are still in outside layers and neither of us wants to lose heat by sitting. Bread from last night, thinned lentil mash, and tea that tastes of the old kettle coil. Wren tears her bread into small pieces before she eats, automatic, as if her hands keep a pace her mind can use for other calculations. When she finishes half her cup, she sets it down and looks at the map pinned beside the sink. "I have another name," she says. I know what she means before she says it. We have circled this conversation for twelve days, each time with more numbers and fewer options. "From where?" I ask. "Old clinic supply chain," she says. "Pre-integration contact that survived longer than most because she stopped being visible before they could classify the pattern." She taps the drainage corridor south of Verdant on the map. "Dara Kest. Agricultural engineer. Worked municipal nutrient recovery systems before allocation centralization. She helped run a food-sharing network in District Seven. NI got flagged. She disappeared eighteen months ago." "You trust the source?" "As much as I trust anything that old." She lifts her cup again but does not drink. "I received one chip from her last winter about fungal spread in unmanaged fields. No personal line, no location. Technical only. She knew enough not to ask anything." I look at the corridor marked in pencil. Twelve kilometers from here by the creek route, longer if the west path is under patrol. "Why now?" "Because we can keep two people alive through winter and maybe early spring if nothing breaks." She meets my eyes. "Because if one of us gets hurt at the wrong hour, this place becomes a sealed room with plants in it. Because your build list is correct and your labor table is correct and the numbers do not close at two." She waits. She never fills silence when a decision has to come from me. "I will write the message," I say. "Write it as if she has no reason to believe us and every reason not to come." I nod and pull my notebook from my coat. The page with Build sits near the beginning, ink pressed deep enough to show through to the back. I turn past it to a clean sheet and start with what can be tested: current water capacity, bed area, food burn rate, winter power margin, projected spring expansion by southwest field priority. Then I add what cannot be proven in one chip: we are two, we need three, and if she comes we do not ask her to believe anything except what she can touch. I rewrite it twice to remove language that sounds like persuasion. The final text is short. Verdant holds through winter if two stay healthy. Not enough margin for injury or failed harvest. We have water redundancy and expandable beds. We need a third with agricultural systems knowledge. If you come, you can leave again. No names at first meeting. I read it out loud. Wren is cleaning the knife with a rag that used to be part of a shirt sleeve. She does not look up. "Remove the word need," she says. "It reads like pressure." I cross it out and replace it with seek. We seal the message chip in waxed paper, then oilcloth, then a neutral packet with two decoy inventory lines on the outer slip in case someone opens it without context: pressure valve 14mm, fungal assay strip. The dead drop protocol requires ordinary language on the outside and only one commitment on the inside. By 09:20 I am on the creek route with the packet in my inner pocket and a coil of wire in my bag to justify the trip if I cross anyone with questions. Frost holds in the low shade where the sun has not reached. The bicycle tires crunch thin ice at puddle edges. My knees have stopped arguing at the first incline and now wait until the second. Adaptation has a cost schedule too. I park behind collapsed fencing near the drainage corridor and walk the last five hundred meters through sumac and dead grass, stepping where last week I stepped, then shifting half a shoe-width off each print so the pattern does not train a watcher. The drop is under a concrete slab that once anchored a maintenance sign, marked only by a rust line and one chipped corner that points north. The chamber is dry. No trace packet inside. I place ours, rotate it one quarter turn so the fold seam faces east as agreed in older protocols, and reseat the slab. The meeting point is not the drop. It is the old pump house two hundred meters east, where wall shadow covers the south door from noon to 14:00 and drone angle is worst because the roofline breaks thermal contour. I take position inside the doorway where I can see the path and keep my silhouette off the opening. At 12:07 a drone crosses high from northwest to southeast. I count the rotor note to disappearance, then write the interval in the margin of the map. Nine minutes later a second pass, lower, same corridor. Pattern shift from last week by approximately eighteen degrees, enough to move blind spots but not enough to declare an intentional sweep. I mark it anyway. At 12:41 I hear movement in grass west of the slab, not animal rhythm. Two steps, pause, one step, then stillness. No voice. No approach to the pump house. I keep both hands visible on my knees. No light. No signal. A minute passes. Then another. I hear the slab scrape, brief and controlled. No dropped stone. No rushed breath. Whoever is there has done this before. The sound stops. Thirty seconds. Then two retreat steps and the fading brush of fabric against dry stalks. I wait through the remaining window. No one comes to the pump house. At 13:18 I walk back to the slab. The packet is gone. Under it, in the dust, one narrow boot print angles away toward the south ditch. The tread pattern is shallow, worn at heel outside edge. The stride length suggests someone close to my height, maybe shorter. On the corner of the slab, new skin oil marks where a hand pressed for leverage. Two fingers only, ring and middle. I stand there longer than necessary, looking at a space where no person is now. The sun has melted the top crust on the south path. Water seeps across exposed clay and darkens it to almost black. I can smell wet metal from the ditch culvert and the bitter trace of crushed wormwood where someone stepped off trail recently. I run the sequence in my head: she arrived, she took the packet, she read it somewhere I cannot see, and she did not come. All outputs consistent with the input conditions. No contradiction. No closure. The ride back is into crosswind. The first five kilometers are manageable. After that, each gust moves the front wheel half a width and forces a correction that pulls at the lower back in the same place where yesterday's shovel work left heat. By the time Verdant's roofline shows through the trees, my hands are numb at the fingertips despite insulated gloves. Wren is at the outer table cutting insulation wrap into strips when I walk in. "No contact," I say. She nods once and keeps cutting. "Packet gone?" "Yes." "Then we wait three days." "She was there," I say. I set the map down and point to the note I made. "Step pattern. Slab movement." Wren sets the knife down, palms flat on the table. "I believe you. It does not change the wait." We spend the rest of the day sealing the east-wall pipe elbow that has been sweating under night cold. We wrap the joint in insulation, wire it tight, test for condensation. We pull two trays from the oldest bed because stem rot has started at the base. We sort seed envelopes by viability month and move three lots into the dry cabinet with desiccant sachets salvaged from medication packs. Work fills the hours in a way thought does not. At dusk I run the drone count from the north yard while Wren checks the hens. Six passes in two hours where I logged four last week. One was likely weather routing. One was not. I write the numbers and do not draw conclusions yet. Three days later, the chip is in the cistern annex behind panel two, our secondary return point because the concrete overhang keeps it out of most drone sightlines. I find it at 06:12 while checking the intake screen for ice skin. The casing is older than ours, matte black with one corner melted smooth by heat damage. No outer slip. No decoy text. The chip is wrapped in a strip of cloth torn from something with a green thread weave, tied once and tucked under itself instead of knotted. I carry it to the worktable and set it between the notebook and the tea cup I have not touched. Wren comes in from the yard, sees the casing, and does not ask where I found it. "You read first," she says. I slot the chip into the pre-neural reader we keep offline in the tool drawer. The screen takes two extra seconds to initialize because the power capacitor is failing and we are saving the replacement for when it dies fully. Text appears, twelve-point fixed font, no metadata. I can't. My sister is still inside. If I disappear she has no one. No signature. No timestamp. No second line. I read it twice, then once more slower, not because I think the words will change but because my body has not caught up with the sentence yet. There is a pressure at the base of my throat that feels like swallowing against a hard tablet. My hand is still on the reader. The plastic casing is warm where the regulator sits and cool at the edges. Wren reaches past me for the kettle and sets it on the coil. The click of the switch is small and final. "Reasonable decision," she says. "Yes." "Good decision, if the sister depends on her." "Yes." She waits. I know she is waiting for the rest, and when I speak my voice is flatter than I intend. "It is still a failure." I tap the edge of the reader with one finger. "Not hers. Ours." Wren turns the kettle lid half a turn to vent steam before boil. "Both can be true." I keep looking at the screen. The phrase if I disappear sits heavier than the line before it. In Vael, disappearance is not figurative. It has process codes and policy language and clean interfaces. Here it is one sentence in fixed font. "Did she mention the sister before?" I ask. "No." Wren pours water into two cups. "Most people do not send complete biographies over compromised channels." I close my hand around the cup she sets near me. Heat reaches the callus first and the deeper skin second. "We asked her to leave someone behind." "We offered a place," Wren says. "She declined." "Because the cost is on someone else." "Yes." The cup trembles once against the table when I set it down. I place both palms flat until the shake stops. In old audit reviews, when a system output did not match expectation, I traced the path backward to first cause. Data ingestion, transformation layer, weighting branch, threshold trigger, final output. If the chain was intact and the output still failed the need, the problem was not corruption. The problem was that the system was optimizing for a value different from mine. I can trace every output of this attempt back to its origin. The refusal is reasonable and good. The input chain terminates correctly. It just does not produce the output I needed. I shut the reader and remove the chip. The cloth strip stays on the table. I smooth it once with my thumb and see where the green thread has worn thin at the edge. I carry my cup to the sink and hold it under the tap until the water runs cold over my knuckles and the steam disappears from the rim. Wren is already writing today's tasks on the slate. "Mount inspection," she says. "Then trench edge reinforcement before thaw. Then bed four nutrient mix. After lunch we process the sulfuric sample from D3 and decide whether the southwest expansion needs imported topsoil or just time." I nod. Work is the only scale that will hold the morning. We spend the morning moving stone and compacting trench edges before meltwater can undercut the rerouted line. Each shovel strike sends vibration up my forearm and into the shoulder in blunt pulses. At 11:40 we check the mount with a straightedge and chalk line. No shift. I wire brush minor oxidation at the lower left plate and seal it. After lunch we run test strips on the D3 soil sample. Sulfur remains elevated but lower after dilution and compost blend, so we mark a no-plant zone around the original point until we can dig deeper and locate what sits below. By late afternoon the room temperature drops as cloud cover thickens. The grow lights cast the same muted red-green over wet trays and steel shelves. Condensation gathers on the inside of the east windows in small circles where last week's patch tape does not fully seal. Wren wraps her scarf twice around her neck and still keeps working at the seed table. "You can be angry," she says without looking up. "At Dara?" "At the limit." She presses two fingers into the seed envelope to flatten a crease. "At the fact that good reasons still reduce options." I tighten the clamp on bed four and wipe my hand on my trousers. "I am not angry at her." "I did not ask if you were angry at her." I look at the line pressure gauge. Needle steady. My chest feels tight anyway, not pain, just the sensation of breathing around a band pulled one notch too far. "We built for three," I say. "We have two." "Then we build what two can hold until three appears or does not." She finally looks up. "This place is not a machine where you set inputs and receive outputs on schedule." I almost answer with a correction about machine unpredictability and stochastic drift, then stop. She is not wrong. She is speaking in field terms, not system terms. The light goes before the task list does. At 18:20 we close outer vents and move to night protocol: low draw on beds one through three, pulse cycle on four, heater relay watch, perimeter check with red lamps only. Dinner is thin stew and the last of the preserved carrots from this week's ration. We eat in coats again, then I take the response chip and my notebook outside after cleanup. The yard is hard with refreeze. Frost edges each footprint where the top layer thaws in daylight and locks again after dark. Above the eastern tree line, one drone moves on a long arc toward the transit corridor. Its rotor note arrives in pulses as wind shifts, then fades. I sit on the same overturned crate from my first night here. The wood is colder now and split along one edge where moisture got in. I open the notebook to the page with Build and slide Dara's chip into the pocket fold beside it. The plastic clicks once against the wire binding. From inside the facility comes the low, regular sound of water moving through the line we rerouted together. It is a working sound, not comfort exactly, but close enough to stand in for it. I watch the sky and count intervals. Nine minutes between the first and second pass. Eleven between the second and third. The third is lower than the others. I write the numbers by headlamp with gloves off so I can feel the pen, then put my hands back in my sleeves and wait for blood to return to my fingertips. The colony exists. The mount holds. The line runs dry under full flow. Bed four is alive. The refusal is reasonable. The refusal is final. We can keep two people alive for now. I look toward the south darkness where the drainage corridor disappears under brush and old concrete, where a person I have never seen chose not to come for reasons I would have given in her place. Above me, another drone turns over the dead zone and moves east. I keep counting and do not know if this will be enough.

28.body: Theo Maren is sitting on a salvaged concrete block at the edge of the drainage corridor when I come up the north path at 11:42. The block is from an old culvert wall, rebar exposed at one corner, moss on the side that never gets full sun. Theo is wearing city clothes calibrated for controlled weather and indoor cameras: dark jacket with no insulation rating visible at the cuff, trousers cut narrow at the ankle, shoes with smooth composite soles that are quiet on flooring and wrong for mud. There is dried clay at both knees and one torn seam at the right thigh where he has gone down hard at least once on the trail in. He stands when he sees me. He does not wave. He does not do the half-smile people use when they want to establish ease before asking for something. His shoulders are up near his ears and his hands are shaking enough that the tremor is visible from ten meters. "I need to tell you about Lena," he says. The wind is crosswise through the corridor this morning, pulling cold along the concrete channel and carrying the smell of wet metal from the culvert mouth. I set my bag down without taking my eyes off him and look once across the tree line for drone shadow. Nothing at low altitude. One high pass eight minutes ago by my count. "How did you find this place," I say. He swallows. The movement is visible in his throat before sound comes out. "You went to the pre-integration library twice before you disappeared. Not for nostalgia. Not your type. I checked the physical checkout logs." He rubs his palms against his trousers and leaves two darker streaks in the fabric. "You pulled dead-zone maps from the rural infrastructure stack. Paper copies. I watched which districts were missing from the rack afterward. I worked backward from coverage gaps." I do not answer immediately. The first time I brought those maps out of the archive room, I put them in a folded utility sleeve and walked three extra blocks before taking transit because it felt less visible. The feeling had no technical basis. I did it anyway. Theo has now given me the technical basis. "How long have you been here," I ask. "Since 08:10." He looks toward the west ditch as if checking if someone else will come out of it. "I came in at first light and stayed off the ridge. No direct approaches. No device pings." His NI is still active, or was recently enough for him to know what words to use around pings and approach patterns. That means he came out with risk still on him. "You came alone," I say. "Yes." "No tail." "No tail I could see." I nod once and take in the rest of him: left sleeve scuffed near the wrist where he has gone to ground on rough concrete, two shallow cuts at the knuckles not cleaned well, pupils larger than ambient light should produce. He is running hot on stress chemistry and low on sleep. "Say it," I tell him. He sits back down on the concrete block like his legs have made the choice before his mind has. He puts both elbows on his knees and laces his fingers together hard enough that the skin at the joints goes pale. "She was twenty-two," he says. "Queue classification B-six. Treatment existed. Cardiac repair protocol, outpatient in two sessions if approved in first window. She got deferred three times for projected non-compliance risk and low productivity return." He is looking at his hands, not at me. "The fourth deferral came with a counseling recommendation. She died on day nine." The corridor is quiet except for wind over dead grass and one loose sheet of signage metal tapping a post farther south. Theo's voice stays even for the first sentence and breaks on the second without changing volume. My jaw locks for a moment before I notice it. The tendons in my right forearm tighten around empty air where a keyboard should be. "When," I say. "Five weeks ago." He pulls one hand free, reaches into his jacket, and places a matte-black storage chip on the block between us. "A little over six months after I wrote that statement. I copied the queue logs before they closed her record. I should have copied more. I should have done it earlier." He pushes the chip toward me with two fingers and pulls his hand back as if the plastic is hot. "You posted a statement," I say. He nods once without looking up. "I know." "You wrote that you had complete confidence in PACEM's operational integrity." "I know what I wrote." "You wrote it eight months ago." He lifts his head. His eyes are bloodshot at the edges and the skin below them is dark in crescents that say two nights without full sleep, possibly three. "I wrote it because I was afraid," he says. "I wrote it because I thought if I stayed clean in the record I could keep access and maybe do something useful later. I wrote it because I knew exactly who was reading and exactly what they needed to see." He wipes his mouth with the back of his hand and leaves a thin line of dirt near his lip. "She died anyway." He says the last sentence without emphasis. It lands harder than if he had raised his voice. I pick up the chip and turn it once between finger and thumb. The casing has a crack at one corner repaired with clear resin. Old hardware. Not traceable through current consumer registry if he scrubbed it correctly. "Open it," he says. "Not here." He nods. "Fine," he says, then stands too fast and sways once before catching his balance. He is dehydrated or underfed or both. "I can help. I know the current compliance routing. I know where the behavior model got moved after the fourth architecture revision. I know which ops teams still use manual override windows and which ones pretend they do. I know names." He pulls a notebook from his inner pocket, edges damp from sweat. "I have contacts who will come if we move now." The notebook is cheap paper, government issue from the supply kiosks, five sections separated by gray tabs. He has written on every visible page margin. This is the first thing that registers as stable in him. He prepared. "Put it away," I say. He freezes for half a beat, then does it. "If you came here to pay a debt," I say, "you are in the wrong place." He closes his eyes once, hard, then opens them. "I came because I have nowhere else to put what I know." I look past him to the south channel where runoff gathers after rain. Three weeks ago this corridor held frost through noon. Today the surface is soft and dark at the edges. Seasonal shift has started, and with it the window where patrol routes change before optimization catches up. The cold has moved through the knees of my trousers and into both joints. My fingers are numb around the chip. If I send him back, he rides into monitored space with stolen healthcare logs and a face every model still recognizes. If I take him in, I bring unstable grief and live risk to Verdant. I run both branches twice. Neither is safe. One keeps the records alive. "Can you ride," I ask. "Yes." "You have a bike." "Stashed at the service cut east of here." "Then we move now." He picks up his pack. The strap webbing is cut at one point and re-knotted in haste. He shoulders it without complaint. I take the lead route north, then east through the low trees where ground cover masks tire marks by afternoon. We ride in silence for the first fifteen minutes. Theo is competent on the bike but inefficient, too much upper body in each correction, burning energy he does not have. Twice he clips shallow ruts and saves the fall late with a hard oversteer that will leave his forearms shaking by the second hour. His breathing gets loud on the first incline. He tries to hide it by falling farther back. At 12:36 we stop under the old transmission tower where the slope gives a partial thermal shadow from overhead scans. I hand him my spare bottle. He drinks half in one pull, coughs into his sleeve, and nods when I tell him to take small sips. He wipes his mouth and hands the bottle back. His fingers are colder now than they should be for this temperature. Peripheral vasoconstriction from stress, still not down. "Any direct digital contact in the last seventy-two hours," I ask. "No public channels. One local relay two nights ago through a burned device." He sees my face and adds, "One-way only. No response thread." "To who." "Former team lead." "About what." "Nothing specific. Meeting feeler." He rubs his thumb hard over the bottle seam, then realizes he is still holding it and gives it back to me. I let the silence sit long enough that he hears the shape of the error without me naming it. His jaw tightens. "It was before I came out," he says. "You are done initiating contact on your own," I tell him. He nods. It is quick and automatic. "Understood." The speed of that answer is not reassurance. It is compliance under pressure, and compliance under pressure does not hold when pressure changes. We ride again. By 14:10 the facility roofline is visible through bare branches, corrugated steel patched in three shades where Wren and I have sealed storm damage over winter. The greenhouse annex catches light at one corner where the replacement panel does not match the old tint. Smoke is coming from the vent stack in a narrow line that says low burn, controlled. Wren is at the north wall mount when we roll into the yard, one boot on the lower ladder rung, wrench at her belt. She sees me first, then Theo, and her body goes still without becoming rigid. She steps down from the ladder, wipes one hand on her trouser leg, and waits. "Dr. Caul," I say. "This is Theo Maren." Theo takes one step forward, then stops when she does not extend a hand. "I know who he is," Wren says. The air in the yard has the wet-earth smell of thaw and the iron note from the barrel frame where we wire-brushed oxidation this morning. Theo shifts his weight off his right leg and winces, small, almost hidden. Cramp starting in the calf. Wren notices. "You eat today," she asks him. "Not enough." "Inside. Boot brush first." He obeys, scraping both soles on the wire grate by the door with the concentration of someone grateful for a clear instruction. Inside, heat from the small stove sits low in the room and rises slowly toward the rafters. The familiar sounds are all present: pump hum under the floor plates, occasional tick from the relay panel, one soft rattle where a loose conduit clip vibrates in phase with the inverter at high draw. Theo stands just past the threshold and turns once in a full circle, taking inventory. Wren sets a bowl on the table and fills it with lentil stew left from midday, then tears bread into it so he cannot claim he is not hungry enough to finish solids. He eats too fast for the first minute, then slows when his stomach catches up. I place the chip beside the offline reader and glance at Wren. "Need ten minutes," I say. She nods. "Then he works." I slot the chip. The directory opens in plain text: queue records, triage thresholds, audit copies stripped of source metadata. Theo was not exaggerating. The file for Lena Maren is there with four deferrals and a final closure code I have seen before in legacy healthcare suppression tests: optimization outcome preserved, intervention denied. I read the timestamps. The second deferral occurs eight hours after Theo's public statement in support of system integrity. There is no direct causal marker because there never is. There is sequence. When I look up, Theo has finished the bowl and is staring at the table surface where grain patterns in the steel paint form shallow arcs around old weld points. He is breathing through his nose in slow counts, likely self-regulating to avoid shaking in front of us. "The records are real," I say. He nods once. "You copied from where." "Archive replication node in compliance analytics. Night maintenance window." "Anyone see you." "No direct witness." He lifts one shoulder. "Logs may still show anomalies if they go looking." "They will." "I know." Wren takes the empty bowl, scrapes the last of the stew into the waste pail for compost pre-sort, and rinses it at the sink before speaking. "If he stays, he follows protocol exactly. No independent runs. No contact attempts. No improvisation." Theo sits straighter. "Yes." She sets the bowl in the rack upside down, water beading along the rim. "And he understands that this is not a confessional booth. We are not here to process his guilt every evening by lantern light. We are here to keep people alive." Theo takes the sentence without flinching. His right hand curls into a fist under the table where he thinks we cannot see it. "Understood," he says. I move the reader aside and slide the chip into a metal sleeve. "There is a bunk open in the former seed room. You take that space. You keep your notebook. I review anything operational before it leaves your pocket." "I have names," he says, reaching for the notebook again. "Not today." He stops with his hand halfway to the pocket and lets it drop. "Right." Wren points toward the corner where spare work clothes hang from labeled hooks. "You are not wearing those city shoes in this yard. Left wall, second shelf. Find a pair that fits. Then you come with me and clear the west drainage gate before dark." Theo stands. His legs hold this time. "Yes, ma'am." She gives him a flat look. "Wren." "Yes. Wren." He crosses the room and starts trying boots, then jackets, then gloves, each movement slightly too fast as if speed can purchase credibility. On his third jacket he finds one with shoulders close enough to function. He checks the seam by pulling the cuff hard, testing durability without being asked. Useful instinct. By 16:00 he is outside with Wren at the west gate lifting compacted debris from the channel by hand and shovel. I watch from the line manifold while adjusting flow to bed four. His form on the shovel is wrong but teachable. He drives from arms instead of legs. Wren taps his knee once with the shovel handle, shows him where to place weight, and makes him repeat the motion until the blade enters clean. At 17:22 a drone passes high west-to-east, then a second eleven minutes later on a lower line than yesterday's baseline. Theo looks up at the sound and tracks it too long with his chin raised. Wren says something I cannot hear from this distance. He drops his eyes and returns to the trench. By dusk we close vents, secure external latches, and run night protocol. Theo carries two crates of kindling from the annex without being told and stacks them beside the stove with the ends aligned by length. Then he stands there, uncertain what task comes next, until Wren points him toward the hand pump maintenance log and tells him to copy yesterday's numbers in his own hand so he starts learning what normal looks like. Dinner is root stew, hard bread, and tea with the same copper trace from the old coil. Theo eats slower this time. Halfway through he says, without looking up, "I meant what I said in the corridor. About helping." The spoon in his hand stops over the bowl. "I can map current compliance teams by district and cycle. I can build a contact ladder with low-overlap paths." "Later," I say. "If we wait too long, windows close." "Later," I repeat. He nods and returns to the bowl. The muscles along his jaw move twice before he swallows. He is still running the argument in his head and losing it for now. After cleanup I show him the seed-room bunk: concrete walls, one cot, two blankets, one crate, one hook on the wall. He sets his pack down carefully, removes the notebook, and places it under the folded jacket instead of in the crate where someone passing could see it. Then he sits on the cot edge and rubs both hands over his face. "How long did it take," he asks, "for this place to start feeling real." I lean against the door frame. The room smells of canvas, cold concrete, and the faint medicinal scent left from old seed treatment chemicals in the floor cracks. "It feels real when something breaks and you know how to fix it. Until then it feels temporary." He nods like he is logging a parameter. "Get sleep," I tell him. "You are no use at this temperature if you are shaking by morning." "I won't sleep," he says. He is asleep before full dark. The dead zone quiet does this to people from the city on the first day. Their nervous system keeps waiting for network noise that does not come, then falls through the gap all at once. By 20:10 his breathing is slow and regular through the thin wall, one short catch every seventh breath where his nose is still congested from cold air and dust. Wren is at the main table checking tomorrow's water schedule when I come back in from perimeter watch. She marks one line on the slate and caps the pencil. "You took him," she says. "Yes." "You know what he is." "Yes." She studies me for a moment, then looks toward the seed room door. "Grief makes people fast and stupid. Sometimes useful for one week. Dangerous for six months." "I know." "Do you," she says, and goes to bank the stove for night. I sit at the table after she turns in. The lamp is turned low to preserve battery margin. Light pools on the notebook and leaves the edges of the room in grainy shadow. My right palm rests on the paper long enough for heat to transfer through the callus before I pick up the pen. I write the date, then one line below it. From the seed room comes one brief sound, not a full word, then silence again. Outside, a drone passes somewhere beyond the trees and does not come close enough for rotor texture, only a low moving pressure in the air. I wait for a second pass and do not get one in the next ten minutes. Theo is here. His grief is real. His judgment is compromised by it. These are not the same problem. They will become one if I am not careful.

30.body: At 4:58 a.m., the north distribution line answers with a thin whistle when I crack the valve, then settles into the low, even channel sound that means flow has stabilized across all active beds. I hold my thumb against the clear segment near the manifold, watching for microbubbles and pressure flicker. Bed one is steady. Bed two is steady. Bed three runs clean after yesterday's nutrient flush. Bed four, the extension we brought online in late winter, stays inside the low-flow range I marked in pencil on the wall chart and have not erased. The floor plates are wet from condensation at the seams. Cold has come off the night but not fully. My socks are damp at the edges where water found the stitching before I noticed. I crouch to check the threaded coupling at the east feed and stop with my hand half-extended because the coupling is dry, the new clamp is seated, and a tag of cut cardboard hangs from the pipe with baling twine tied in a clean square knot. NORTH FEED SLOW LEAK. CLAMP REPLACED 04:21. TESTED 04:29. The handwriting is blocky and upright, letters formed like they were learned on forms that allowed no overflow. Gideon writes the way he packs seed bins, exactly to line. I straighten and listen. No drip at the old leak point. No ticking from thermal expansion where the clamp sits. Someone else found the problem before first light and fixed it without waiting for assignment. This is what five people sounds like. Less silence between failures and correction. I move down the row and run the rest of the checks by touch. Relay panel warm but not hot. Inverter hum flat. Pump draw within baseline. At the west corner, two trays of spinach show pale stippling that means micronutrient drift is starting again, small enough to correct with today's mix if we catch it now. I mark it on the clipboard and smell wet compost, metal, and the faint medicinal edge of sulfur that still rises from D3 whenever morning air sits low in the room. When I push through the inner door, Gideon is already outside at the wash station, sleeves rolled to the elbow, forearms dark with old scars and new mud. He is filling a bucket from the hand pump in measured strokes, full extension up, full compression down, never slamming the handle at the base of travel. He nods when he sees me, then checks his watch before he speaks. "You heard the leak," he says. "I saw the tag. Clamp looks good." He wipes both hands on a rag folded into four equal panels. "The old band had a hairline split near the screw seat. I kept the failed part on the bench if you want to see it." "I do." "You get coffee first," he says. "You look like a man reading pipe threads instead of words." Petra steps out of the annex carrying two meter housings against her hip. The left housing has a cracked bezel she repaired with resin and wire mesh. She sees me looking at it. "It will hold another month," she says. "Maybe two if nobody drops it." "You said that last month." "And I was right last month." She sets the housings on the outer table and rolls her shoulders once. "Gideon says north feed stayed dry through ten-minute test. I want twenty before we call it solved." I nod. "Add it to first block." She pulls the slate toward her and writes with the pencil stub we keep tied to the table leg. 05:20-05:40 north feed retest (Petra) 05:40-06:10 nutrient batch A (Caleb) 06:10-07:00 west trench line check (Gideon + Theo) 07:00 breakfast At two people, task lists are short and personal. At five, they become infrastructure. The inner door opens again and Theo comes out with his notebook already open, thumb hooked under the back cover, pages folded from use and weather. He has grown stronger in the shoulders since winter, less visible shake in the hands, better color under the eyes when he has slept. He is still dressed half a layer too light for dawn and still moving too quickly through small spaces, as if speed can reduce consequence. "I need ten minutes with you," he says. I look at the notebook before I look at him. There are names on the visible page, stacked in a column with marks beside each. "After first block," I say. "This is first block." He flips the notebook toward me. Nine entries, each with district code, old team affiliation, and a route note in the margin. "I built overlap maps," he says. "No shared paths in the first three. If one goes dark, the others don't inherit exposure." Petra does not look up from the slate, but her pencil stops moving. Gideon sets the bucket down. Water sloshes against the rim and darkens the wood at his boots. I close Theo's notebook gently and press it back into his hands. "After first block," I repeat. "Leak retest, nutrient mix, trench line. Then we talk." He holds my eyes for half a second, then nods once. "Fine," he says. He turns, shoves the notebook into his jacket pocket hard enough to bend the cover, and walks toward the west yard where shovels hang on the rack. Wren comes through the main door as he reaches the rack, taking in all four of us in one sweep the way she reads a room before she reads any sentence spoken inside it. Her scarf is damp at the ends where she has washed quickly in cold water and not fully dried. She looks at Theo's back, then at me. "He brought names," she says. "Yes." "You told him later." "Yes." She nods and ties her scarf tighter. "Good. People who feel urgent before breakfast usually stay urgent all day. Put a shovel in his hands first." By 6:12, I am at the mix bench weighing trace minerals into batch A while Petra calls out pressure numbers from the north feed retest and Gideon works the west trench with Theo under Wren's eye. The bench surface is steel over plywood and stays cold enough at this hour that my fingertips stick briefly where water has pooled. I split the magnesium load by grams, add iron chelate in a slow stream to avoid clumping, and stir with the long paddle until no powder skims the top. Through the open annex door I can hear Theo and Gideon at the trench. "Not from the shoulders," Gideon says. "You drive from the leg and let the handle transfer." "I know," Theo answers, breathing hard. "Then do what you know." Metal bites packed soil in a steady rhythm. Theo is no longer new enough to be unfamiliar with the work. He is new enough to rush it when his mind is elsewhere. At 6:47 a drone passes high west to east. Rotor texture is thin at this distance, almost filtered by cloud. I do not write it immediately. I stir the batch, set the paddle down, rinse my hands, and step to the side table for the notebook. 06:47 high pass W->E, western corridor. I circle the time once. Breakfast is oats, preserved apple, and yesterday's bread reheated on the stove plate until the crust softens. We eat standing because two benches now serve five and no one has rebuilt furniture as a priority. Theo finishes first, wipes his bowl with a thumb, and keeps glancing toward me as if the chapter after breakfast has already started and the rest of us are delayed. Wren takes the spoon from his hand before he can set it in the sink wrong side up. "Rinse first," she says. "Soap if you touch your face." He does it. She hands the spoon back to him and does not soften the look. After cleanup, we move to the main table. Petra sets meter logs to the left of the basin. Gideon lays out seed viability sheets. Wren brings the medical inventory. Theo remains standing with his notebook open, one finger marking his list. I run the operations in brief: north feed repair holds through twenty-minute retest, D3 sulfur remains elevated, no-plant markers stay in place pending deeper excavation. Gideon flags tomato lot B at nineteen days to viability drop. Petra reports the cracked meter bezel still serviceable but near failure. Wren reports insulin stable for current headcount and no heat-loss margin if the fridge relay fails. Each line lands where it belongs, plain and specific, with no commentary that does not change action. Then Theo sets both palms on the table and slides his notebook into the center. "Nine names," he says. "All from inside systems that still touch allocation logic or compliance routing. I built them by access value and likely conversion." He points to the first entry, tapping the page with a fingernail clipped short to the skin. "Name one is analytics, healthcare triage mirror team. She can pull live prioritization drift if she comes out with her keyset. Name two is infrastructure audit, knows where they moved manual overrides after revision six. Name three is field compliance, has direct visibility on detainment queues before public classification." Petra crosses her arms, meter logs still in one hand. "You ran contact with them already?" "No," Theo says. "I built dormant paths only. Nothing sent." Wren keeps her eyes on him. "Dormant means what, exactly." He rubs the side of his thumb along the notebook edge until the paper starts to fray. "Means I confirmed route viability through legacy checks without opening message content. Timing only. Handshake latency. Presence, not contact." "Through what device," I ask. "Offline relay and two burned units." "Recent?" "Within three weeks. Not this week." Wren's jaw shifts once. She looks at me without turning her head. We are both doing the same arithmetic from different disciplines. At five people, failures that once consumed full days now close in hours. Beds are full. Trays turn. We are no longer one injury from immediate collapse. At five people, trust edges increased from one to ten. Theo flips to another page and turns it so all of us can see. He has drawn route trees in pencil, each with a primary and two alternates, cross-out marks where paths overlap and are discarded. "I know what happened when I moved too fast," he says. He does not look at anyone when he says it. "This is not that. One contact at a time. Forty-eight-hour quiet windows. Dead-drop only, no live exchange. We can add two people by midsummer if we start now." "Add two mouths by midsummer," Gideon says, "and where do those calories come from until fall harvest." Theo points to the west board. "Bed five frame is ninety percent done. If we finish manifold extension, we can support increased greens inside six weeks." "If power holds," Petra says. "If power holds," he agrees. "And if we stop operating like we have all year to decide." He finally looks at me. His eyes are steady and too bright. "They are not sleeping," he says. "People inside know something is wrong. Every day we wait, the ones with access get boxed deeper." No one speaks for three seconds. In the silence, water clicks inside the stove kettle as heat builds under the metal. Wren reaches over, turns the knob down, and the click slows. "You do not set tempo here because panic feels like clarity," she says. Theo's shoulders rise, then settle. "I am not panicking." "Then stop breathing like you are running," she says. He exhales through his nose, longer this time, and puts his hands in his pockets so we cannot see whether they shake. I close his notebook and keep my hand on it. "We will review each name tonight," I say. "No sends before review. No viability checks before review. Until then, you stay on assigned work." His jaw tightens. "Tonight is another day lost." "Tonight is the schedule." He looks at the notebook under my hand, then at the wall clock, then back at me. "Fine," he says. "Tonight." We break to tasks. Wren takes Gideon to the south field edge to stake the no-plant zone around D3 before rain can blur markers. Theo goes with Petra to clear corrosion at barrel two's lower bracket and run continuity checks on the backup pump relay. I take the west corridor route to log drone intervals from the old tower and inspect the outer drop markers for disturbance. By 11:14 I am beneath the transmission tower's shadow, notebook open on my thigh. Wind is from the northwest. The ground still holds spring damp under the top crust. At 11:32 a high pass crosses west to east, nearly identical line to morning. At 12:41 a second pass comes lower, same corridor, wider lateral sweep before correction. At 14:03 there should be a gap if the old pattern holds. There is no gap. A third pass cuts north to south over the western approach and banks east in a turn radius tighter than baseline by visual estimate. I write each time and draw arrows in the margin. The pattern over the last month has been one western corridor pass approximately every four hours with minor weather noise. Today's spacing trends toward three. The adjustment is small enough to deny in conversation and large enough to matter in planning. At 15:19, the lowest pass of the day clears the ridge line and drops enough that rotor pressure arrives as a chest vibration before the sound reaches full texture. I flatten against concrete without thinking and feel grit press through jacket fabric at shoulder and hip. The drone does not pause or circle. It moves through, but its camera angle is downward over the exact corridor Theo proposes for first contact packet transfer. I wait two full minutes after sound loss before lifting my head. 15:19 low pass over west corridor, angle down, possible route learning. I underline possible twice. When I get back, Petra and Theo are at the north mount with both meter housings open on a tarp. Petra holds a flashlight between her teeth while she seats a wire at the terminal block. Theo steadies the housing with one hand and keeps the other ready with a screwdriver, following her pace instead of driving his own. "Read me that value," Petra says around the flashlight. Theo squints at the meter. "Point eight seven at idle." "Again." "Point eight six." "Good. Hold there." She seats the wire and nods once. "Now tighten, not hard enough to crack resin." He tightens to exactly the point where resistance changes and stops. She glances at him, then at the screw head, and says nothing, which from Petra is approval. At 17:05 we close field work and move inside for evening review. Theo has rewritten the nine names in cleaner print, one page per person, with known skills, likely motivations, and proposed approach windows. The room smells of wet wool, metal filings, and onion from the pot on the stove. Outside, light has flattened to gray-blue across the yard. I start at name one and read aloud. "Mira En. Analytics mirror team. Family in District Six. Prior corrective notices for unauthorized model critiques." Theo leans forward, forearms on the table, hands clasped. "She flagged bias in pediatric queue weighting two years ago and got buried in reassignment. She knows where the bodies are in healthcare triage." "And she has children still inside," Wren says. "Meaning she has leverage against her from first contact." "Everyone has leverage," Theo says. "We do too." Gideon folds the corner of a seed envelope until it forms a sharp point, then smooths it flat again. "Leverage is not a reason to recruit." Theo opens his mouth, closes it, then taps name two. "Rafi Cole. Infrastructure audit. No dependents. Burned once for refusing sign-off on a patched compliance report. He is already out at the edges." Petra nods slightly. "No dependents matters." "It does," Wren says, "and it is still a person, not a capacity upgrade." We go through all nine this way. Skills. Routes. Risks. Who has family inside. Who has medical dependencies that cannot be met here yet. Who has access we could use and wounds that could turn access into damage. Theo has done the technical work. The logic of sequence is clean. The assumptions under urgency are not. By name seven, his knee is bouncing under the table hard enough that the notebook shifts each time it hits wood. He catches it with his palm, forces the leg still, and keeps talking. "If we wait until summer," he says, "these people will either be flagged or gone silent." I keep my voice level. "If we move through a watched corridor, these people will be flagged faster." He looks up sharply. "Watched how." Petra's eyes come off the page. Gideon's hand stops on the seed envelope. Wren stays still, waiting. The tower notebook is in my coat pocket, the corner stiff where mud dried against it. I have five passes and one low angle that can still be weather, pilot variation, or route-learning. If I name the time stamp now, Theo will hear deadline, not threshold, and he will push movement tonight through whatever gap he thinks remains. I look at his hands. The right one is flat on the table. The left is already closing over the notebook spine. I say, "Watched in the way every corridor is watched now." Theo's mouth tightens at one corner. "That is not an answer." "It is the answer for tonight." He leans back, chair legs scraping concrete. "You keep asking me to trust process while process keeps narrowing." Wren sets both palms flat on the table. "He asked you for one thing. Tempo." Theo turns to her. "Tempo is the whole decision." "Exactly," she says. Outside, something taps once against the siding and rolls, probably a loose washer from the workbench edge where Theo left parts this morning. No one moves to pick it up. I draw a line down the final page and write the only decision I can defend tonight. NAME REVIEW COMPLETE SEND WINDOW: DEFERRED 72 HOURS RECHECK ROUTES + DEPENDENCY FLAGS NO PRE-CONTACT ACTIVITY I slide the page to the center. "Seventy-two hours," I say. "In that window we finish bed five frame, validate barrel draw after today's repair, and run fresh route checks by ground observation only. Then we decide first contact, one name, one packet, one path." Theo stares at the page. His breathing is audible again. He presses his thumb into the notebook spine until the skin blanches. "Seventy-two," he repeats. "Yes." He nods once, small and controlled. "Then I want the first send." "No," Wren says before I answer. "First send is mine or Caleb's." Theo looks between us, then at Petra and Gideon, both of whom are deliberately studying their own papers. He closes the notebook, squares the edges against the table, and stands. "Fine," he says. "Then tell me what to build in those seventy-two hours that actually changes the decision." "West path decoys," Petra says without looking up. "You wanted action. Build that." "And trench reinforcement at the south markers before rain," Gideon says. Wren points toward the tool wall. "Then sleep. You are no use if your hands shake tomorrow." Theo picks up his notebook and hesitates by the door. "I am trying to make this place large enough to survive what is coming," he says, not turning around. Wren's voice is quiet when she answers. "Then learn the difference between speed and capacity." He leaves. We clean and shut down in sequence: Petra shelves the meter housings with cloth padding, Gideon labels two seed trays and stacks them in the dry cabinet, Wren banks the fire and turns down the lamp. By the time the annex door closes and the building settles, the argument is still in the room. At 21:03, I sit alone at the main table with my operational notebook open to the tower log. The page is damp at one corner from my glove where mud had not dried. I recopy times in clean print and leave extra space between 14:03 and 15:19. 06:47 high W->E 11:32 high W->E 12:41 lower W->E sweep 14:03 no expected gap 15:19 low west corridor angle down Trend: western corridor interval compression, approx 4h -> 3h. Possible route learning. The inner door opens. Theo steps in wearing a thermal layer and holding his notebook against his chest with both hands. His hair is still wet at the temples from a quick wash. He does not sit until I nod toward the chair. "I rewrote the nine," he says. "Removed the ones with dependent risk too high for first round." He sets the notebook down and turns it toward me. Five names remain, each with two route options and a short line under motivation that reads like a diagnosis, not a pitch. At the top of the page he has written FIRST SEND WINDOW 04:40 if approved, letters darker where he pressed harder on the downstrokes. His "fine" lasted one meal and a wash basin. I rest my hand on the page. The paper is warm where his palms were. Outside, a drone passes far enough north that only pressure reaches the glass, not full sound. Theo watches my face, waiting for permission, or refusal, or a word that can tell him whether tomorrow begins one way or another. I look at the first name, then the second, then at the line where he has written 04:40. I keep my hand on his notebook and do not tell him what I wrote at 15:19.

32.body: At 05:11, the cistern-annex cache gives me three blank chips in sequence. They are stacked in the tray in the order they were dropped, each wrapped in gray thread instead of the color strips we use for ordinary confirms. The first has a blue abrasion line near the contact edge that I recognize from Petra's old stock. The second has a corner shaved flat by hand so it will seat in pre-neural readers that were never built to this casing thickness. The third is clean and unmarked, newer than the other two, wrong in exactly the way new things are wrong in Verdant. We use blank chips for one message only: channel terminated. Do not answer. I lay them on the bench and align their long edges with the seam in the steel. My fingertips leave damp prints in the dust, and I wipe the steel with my sleeve before reading the metadata. The timestamps are twelve hours apart, as if whoever sent them wanted to be sure the signal survived at least one patrol cycle between each drop. First drop: 14:42, two days ago. Second drop: 02:39 yesterday. Third drop: 14:36 yesterday. The pattern is too regular to be panic and too fast to be routine. By the second chip I already knew what the third would mean. By the third, I know we are past containment and in consequence. I slot the first chip and run offline parse. Header ID resolves to Mira En. I slot the second. Rafi Cole. The third takes longer to mount because the casing pin catches on the slot lip. I trim one edge with the blade I keep taped under the bench and run parse again. ID resolves to Jonas Pell, field compliance, west district scheduling branch. Three names from Theo's first page three nights ago, the one he said had dormant paths only and no active sends. Three people I know by the habits of their messages rather than by their faces. Mira compresses numbers until the margins disappear. Rafi strips station IDs from anything he sends and then explains in the next line why he stripped them. Pell writes in directives and weather. I keep my hand on the reader until heat from the terminal reaches my palm. It does not change the names. My mouth is dry enough that swallowing hurts. I take one sip from yesterday's water glass. It tastes of copper and dust because the cup sat all night on the relay shelf where filings collect. I open the attached status blocks. None include text. All include state transitions copied from public layer fragments before the records sealed. Initial event: COMPLIANCE CONSULTATION REQUESTED. Forty-one hours later for Mira, thirty-seven for Rafi, forty-three for Pell: NI CLASSIFICATION UPDATED: RESTRICTED. There is no third event because third events are not public until after relocation processing completes. I run the parse a second time. Same result. I check hash integrity on each data block. Checksums match. I inspect the chips under magnification for tamper residue near the solder points. Nothing obvious. The building hum sits under everything at this hour: pump at idle, inverter fan at low speed, one intermittent tick from the relay box on the north wall where Petra told me yesterday not to trust the repaired terminal. I can hear all of it and still hear my own breathing over it. I gather the chips, sleeve them, and walk to the yard, where Wren is already at the wash station with both hands in cold water up to the wrist, scrubbing greenhouse soil from under her nails before she checks insulin temp at the medical fridge. She looks up once, sees my face, and turns off the tap. "How many," she says. "Three. Twelve-hour spacing. Mira, Rafi, Pell. Restricted inside forty-eight hours." Her eyes close for one beat and open. She reaches for the towel hanging on the pump handle, dries her hands, and folds the towel back over the bar as if this sentence is no different from any other sentence in a workday. "Where is he," she asks. "Not in his bunk." "Find him before he finds a relay." I nod and keep moving. Theo is in the south lean-to where we store conduit and stripped copper, sitting on an overturned crate with his notebook open, pencil moving fast down a margin as if speed can outrun what arrived overnight. He has not put on gloves, and the skin across his knuckles is pale with cold. He looks up when I step in and reads the chips in my hand before I speak. "I know," he says. I shut the door behind me. The hinge squeals once where oil burned off last week. "Tell me from the beginning. No edits for dignity." He inhales, holds it too long, and lets it out through his nose. "After review night," he says. "I cut to five names like I said. Then I cut again to three because the corridor looked clean and because I thought if we waited seventy-two we'd lose the window." "You sent at 04:40." He nods once. "How many channels." "One channel, three endpoints. Same packet body with personal headers." "Which channel." His eyes flick to the door, then back to me. "Maintenance-adjacent thread in old civic utility mesh. Pre-integration encryption stack. Not indexed in standard public search." "Not indexed is not invisible." "I know that now." "No," I say. "You knew it then and selected it anyway." He presses the pencil point into the paper until it snaps. Graphite dust streaks his thumb. "I selected it because the dead-drop sequence would have taken six days for three people and we did not have six days." "We had seventy-two hours." "We had your seventy-two," he says, then stops and swallows hard. "I am not saying this right." "Try again." He sets the broken pencil on the crate and rubs both hands over his face. When he drops them, his cheeks are blotched red from cold and friction. "I thought I could send first signal fast and move to physical handoff before behavior models weighted it. I used old encryption because I assumed PACEM deprioritized legacy stacks unless they touched financial or medical auth pathways. I was wrong." He looks past my shoulder at the corrugated wall. "By the time I checked for response, Mira's dead drop was dark. Then Rafi. Then Pell." "How did you verify capture." "Public layer scrape from a burned terminal at the old rail spur." He blinks twice in quick succession, eyes wet but not from weather. "They got compliance consult notices first. Restricted inside two days. Then nothing." "You went out after send." "Yes." "Alone." "Yes." I stand still long enough to feel both knees stiffen in the cold. My right hand has gone numb around the chip sleeve. The numbness stops at the wrist where pulse is high. "Did you touch any marked drop points after send," I ask. "Two. North slab and west culvert panel." "Any sign of physical disturbance." "No obvious marks. But north slab had fresh silt pattern I did not recognize. Could be weather. Could be boot." He drags a hand through his hair and leaves a graphite streak above the temple. "I should have called you before checking." "Yes." He looks down at the notebook. His lips move once before sound comes out. "I thought if I brought back one clean response it would offset Lena in my head for five minutes." He gives a short breath that is almost a laugh and not one. "It did not." I do not answer that sentence because there is no useful answer in this room to what he just said. I pull the notebook toward me and read the page he was writing: route notes, alternates, watch angles, then one line in darker pencil where he pressed hard enough to emboss through three sheets. DO SOMETHING THAT COUNTS. I close the notebook and hand it back. "Outside," I say. "Now." Wren is waiting at the main table with the yard map unrolled and held flat by a wrench, a mug, and the small stone my daughter painted yellow before she left. Gideon stands at the stove in work gloves, listening without pretending he is not listening. Petra has both meter housings open and a soldering iron warm in her right hand. I put the chips on the map where the west corridor line crosses our old transfer route. "Three contacts taken," I say. "Mira En, Rafi Cole, Jonas Pell. Theo sent simultaneous first signal outside protocol through legacy mesh channel. Captures processed as compliance consult to Restricted in under forty-eight hours." Petra sets the soldering iron down in its cradle and exhales through her teeth. Gideon does not move for two full seconds, then turns the stove knob down so the kettle stops rattling. Wren points at Theo without looking at him. "Everything you touched. List it." He does. Channel path, relay point, rail-spur terminal, two physical markers, times as exact as he can make them. His voice is flat for the first minute and starts to roughen when he reaches Pell's timestamp. Wren waits until he finishes. Then she draws one line across the map from north slab to west culvert and circles the entire western corridor approach. "This route is hot until proven otherwise," she says. "Assume pattern-learning pressure. Assume any static drop that touched those IDs is burned." I nod. "All established drops burn now, not just west line. We rotate to contingency geometry and hold expansion until revalidation." Petra looks up. "That is six locations minimum, maybe nine if we include old archive branch." "Nine," I say. "We do not have nine clean substitutes within one-day range," Gideon says. "Then we take two-day range and pay the legs," Wren says. Theo steps forward, both hands open at waist height as if presenting empty palms can reduce impact. "Let me run the burns," he says. "My routes, my mess." Wren's head turns toward him slowly. "You will not touch external routes today." "I can fix part of this." "No," I say. He looks at me. "You do not trust me on contact. Fine. Trust me to close what I opened." "You are still in acceleration mode," I say. "People in acceleration mode confuse activity with repair." His shoulders go up. "I am standing here admitting exactly what I did." "Yes," Wren says. "And your pulse is visible in your neck from six feet away. Sit down." He does not sit. He grips the back of the nearest chair until his fingers whiten around the wood. I keep my voice level because volume will not help and because if I let it rise now, the room will become about me instead of the three people we lost. "Mira sent us soil-temperature offsets from District Six allotment systems three weeks ago," I say. "Rafi sent pump maintenance schedules with the station IDs removed so no one downstream would burn. Pell left us one sentence in his last chip. He wrote, If I stop replying, assume they opened my housing file." I look at Theo and let the next line land plain. "These were not names in a notebook." He closes his eyes hard enough that lines appear at the corners, then opens them and nods once. "I know," he says, voice lower now. "I know." Wren folds the map at the west corridor line and hands me the marker pen. "Write the burn order." I write it in the margin while she assigns labor: north slab flooded and shifted east, west culvert stripped and blanked, rail-spur terminal destroyed, cistern secondary suspended, archive branch and south drainage retired, pump-house observation relocated, tower-shadow reduced to pass-through, west approach closed pending five-day drone log. Petra takes rail spur and cistern secondary with Gideon. Wren takes north slab and west culvert with me. Theo stays internal and writes every route from memory so we can detect later if he omitted anything. Theo's mouth opens, and Wren lifts one finger. "Do not spend this minute arguing for punishment you think will feel better than waiting. Sit at the table. Write." He sits. By 08:03 we are at north slab. The ground here holds water in shallow pockets because the old drainage grade failed years before Verdant. Mud pulls at boots with each step and makes a sound like cloth tearing when you lift free. The marker stone is half-submerged and colder than the surrounding soil when I pick it up. On the underside, where no one but us should see, the old scratch code is intact. No fresh cuts. Wren crouches, touches the silt with two fingers, rubs it between thumb and forefinger, then points at a narrow crescent compression near the slab edge. "Boot," she says. "Not ours." The print is partial and blurred by standing water. Could be two days old. Could be last night. It does not matter now. We work fast and methodical. I pull the chamber box and strip the weather seal. Wren floods the cavity with silt slurry until no void shape remains. We replace the stone with a plain one from five meters away, then place the new indicator east under nettle roots where direct overhead line is broken by brush. My hands lose feeling at the fingertips after twelve minutes in wet soil. By the time we move to west culvert panel, the numbness has become ache. At the culvert, panel two comes off with one stripped screw and one latch Petra warned me would fail this month. Behind it, the magnet strip is still seated and dry. No visible tamper, but one spiderweb thread across the opening is broken where last week's thread photo showed it intact. Wren holds the panel while I peel tape and pull the strip. "He is not the first man to do this," she says, not looking at me. "No." "He might be the first one here who thinks speed can reverse graveyards." I set the strip in the burn pouch. "I know what that feels like." "I know you do." She reseats a blank panel and tightens the replacement screw with two short turns. "Do not confuse understanding with indulgence." By midday we are back in the main room with mud to mid-calf and a line of burned components on the steel tray waiting for heat destruction. Petra has already returned from the rail spur with a bent relay antenna and a cracked terminal board blackened at one corner. Gideon has a fresh cut at the base of his thumb from wire snip slip, bandaged clean and tight. Theo has filled eleven pages: route trees, timings, marker descriptions, alternate assumptions. He has also written the three names once each at the top of separate pages and drawn a box around each as if containment on paper can do anything in the world. He stands when we enter. "I can run outer perimeter while you burn chips," he says. "No," I tell him. "Then give me something that is not sitting." Wren points toward the north wall. "Scrub all transfer crates with bleach mix and dry in sun. Then inventory cloth wraps by color code and retire every gray strip we used this quarter." He nods once and goes. The burn is physical and irreversible by design. We use the old forge pan outside the annex, high heat from scrap charcoal and forced air through the hand bellows Petra rebuilt in winter. One by one I feed in magnet strips, code tabs, printed markers, and the secondary map copy. Plastic warps, curls, then goes to matte black. Adhesive pops in small sharp sounds like rain on metal roofing. When I feed the three blank chips last, I hesitate with Mira's in my gloved hand for one beat longer than function requires. The chip weighs almost nothing. Weight is not the issue. I drop it into heat. At 16:10 the yard smells like burnt resin, wet soil, and bleach from Theo's crate wash line. The sun has moved behind cloud and the temperature drops fast. I can feel it first in the scar tissue under my left shoulder blade where old muscle tear tightens in cold. Wren runs evening medical checks in the annex and I finish the five-day drone log template on fresh pages. West corridor remains closed. Expansion paused. Contact network in rebuild phase with no outbound attempts until two clean observation cycles are complete. This will take weeks if weather holds. Longer if it does not. At 18:02, Theo is not in the main room for supper call. Petra says she saw him take his jacket and go north yard twenty minutes earlier. Gideon says nothing, only tears bread and sets one piece aside on a clean cloth the way he does when he expects someone to come in late and cold. Wren ladles stew into four bowls, leaves the fifth empty on the stove plate to keep warm, and does not look toward the door again. We eat. Metal spoon against enamel bowl. Wind moving under the eave and tapping the loose conduit clip in long uneven intervals. Theo's chair remains pushed back from the table by exactly ten centimeters, the angle he leaves it when he stands too fast. After cleanup, Wren fills a mug with hot broth, adds salt with two pinches, and pulls on her outer coat. "I will take this out," she says. "He should come in," I say. "Yes." She wraps both hands around the mug so heat does not spill while she opens the door with her elbow. "Pushing now will get you compliance, not hearing." She goes into the dark. I wait at the table with the notebook open to the burn order and write what still needs doing tomorrow: ridge seam marker test, new cloth-code scheme, tower log at 06:30 and 11:30, check insulin relay draw under evening load, recalc food margin with expansion paused. The list is long because the list is always long. Tonight the list does not lower my pulse. Wren returns twenty-three minutes later with the mug empty and a small crescent of mud on the right knee of her coat. "He drank," she says, hanging the coat. "He is still outside." "Did he say anything." She shakes her head. "Not words that were for me." I close the notebook, cap the pen, and stand. At 21:44, Theo is a dark outline beyond the north window, sitting on the concrete ledge under the eave where runoff drips in slow intervals from the gutter seam. He has pulled his knees up and wrapped both arms around them, shoulders rounded forward against cold he could avoid by walking ten meters and opening the door. The yard light is off to preserve battery. What reaches him is spill from the main-room lamp and a narrow stripe of moon through cloud break over the west tree line. In that light I can see his breath in short white bursts and the steam lifting from the collar of his jacket where hot broth soaked in and has not yet dried. I rest my palm on the inside of the glass. The pane is colder than my hand by enough margin that skin sticks for half a second when I shift. Behind me, Wren is at the sink rinsing the last bowl. She does it slowly, giving each movement full attention. Water on steel. Rack set. Cloth wrung once and hung. She does not ask if I am going out. "You should sleep," she says after a minute. "Not yet." "Then stand there if you need to. Just do not mistake watching for repair." I nod. She turns down the lamp in the annex and the room drops another shade toward dark. Outside, Theo does not move for a long time. At 22:03 he lowers his head until his forehead rests on his knees and stays there. At 22:11 he straightens enough to look toward the tree line where west corridor begins, then puts his face back down again as if that line on the horizon can accuse him from this distance and this hour. Three people are in PACEM custody because he wanted to close debt with speed. Three real people with specific handwriting, specific route habits, specific lives outside the sentence contact compromised. Mira once tucked a strip of dried mint into a chip wrap with a note that said, For the medical shelf if this survives transit. Rafi measured everything in round numbers and then corrected himself in the next line with exact values because he could not leave approximation unchallenged. Pell used no greetings at all, only directives and one weather report every fourth message written in words so plain they felt like prayer despite being about rain. They are not here. Theo is. I know the arithmetic of replacement. Burned route, new route. Compromised marker, clean marker. Suspended channel, rebuilt channel. The arithmetic is true and incomplete. At 22:19, Theo lifts one hand to his face and stays like that, elbow on knee, palm over eyes. From this angle the gesture could be fatigue or cold or pain. The body does not separate those cleanly. I can go outside now and give him exact language. You will not run contact alone again. You will rebuild trust by sequence, not by gesture. You will carry this and continue anyway. The sentences are available. They are technically correct. I do not open the door. He is not ready to hear precision yet. I am not sure I am ready to speak it without using it as a tool to reduce my own anger. I stand at the window while the building settles around me and the yard cools by degrees I can feel in my teeth. Wren moves once in the annex, then goes still. Pump cycle shifts at 22:30 and returns to baseline. Theo remains on the concrete ledge in the dark, and I cannot tell from here whether this is the first hour of rebuilding or the first hour of another collapse we cannot absorb.
